title: #eng-infra: 6472105432
preview:
Micah Nguyen: heads-up — after the 03:30 rolling restart window I saw a tempstore IO latency spike in us-west1, GPU attaches started failing with attach timeouts and a bunch of pending pods. Stacktrace-ish logs attached below. Anyone seen this pattern?

Ava Chen: :eyes: can you paste the iostat / dstat snippet? also which janitor/cleaner cron ran?

Micah Nguyen: iostat 1 5 showed crazy await on nvme0n1p1:
```
Device:            tps    kB_read/s    kB_wrtn/s    kB_read    kB_wrtn
nvme0n1           50.0       1200.0       9800.0       6000      49000
```
And kube attach logs: `AttachVolume timed out after 120s waiting for device` — lots of tempfile cleanup activity in syslog. Cron job `janitor-sweep` started 03:32 on many nodes (we rolled 03:25-03:40).

Dmitri Petrov: rolling and janitor overlapped — we had the janitor schedule at */5 during the deploy patch, my bad. It forks up to 12 workers by default. Config snippet: `JOBS=12` in /etc/janitor/config.

Marta Lopez: that explains it. The janitor does full readdir + unlink loops on /tmp/scratch, which blows out small-block IOPS. We should throttle it during cluster restarts. Suggestion: set MAX_WORKERS=2 and add backoff.

Ryan O'Connor: Agree. Also saw provisioner queues back up; autoscaler then requested more Dedicated GPUs to compensate for timeouts, so cost blip. We need a short mitigation to stop autoscaler panic.

Micah Nguyen: mitigation plan proposal:
- temp: scale down janitor workers to 1 on impacted nodes (`sed -i 's/JOBS=12/JOBS=1/' /etc/janitor/config && systemctl restart janitor`)
- patch: add a lockfile so janitor skips if node recently restarted (`/var/run/node-reboot.lock`)
- infra: add Prometheus alert for sustained nvme await > 50ms for >2m to gate autoscaler triggers
- ops: rollback only if attach errors > 10% for 5m

Ben Shah: I can roll the sed+restart across the affected ASG now. Which tag set?

Micah Nguyen: tag = fleet:gpu-temp1, region=us-west1, pool=scratch-high. Start with 10 nodes and monitor.

Ava Chen: quick Prometheus query to watch: `avg_over_time(node_disk_io_time_seconds_total{device="nvme0n1"}[2m]) * 1000` — plot that and hook to alert. Also setting autoscaler cooldown to 8m would reduce flapping.

Dmitri Petrov: I'll change janitor config repo to default MAX_WORKERS=2 and add a heartbeat file check. Will open PR and backport patch to current deploys.

infra-bot: alert: nvme_await_high_us-west1 - 03:34 - firing on 12 nodes. links: https://grafana/redwood/d/abcd/nvme-io?from=...

Marta Lopez: long-term: we should move small-file GC off-host into a queue/worker pool that respects node IO pressure. Also consider async unlink or batching unlinks to reduce metadata churn.

Ryan O'Connor: let's also add a temporary autoscaler suppression label `suppress-autoscale: temp-io` that prevents scale-ups for 10m when set by ops. We can use it as a circuit-breaker while janitor repairs land.

Micah Nguyen: accepted. Ben please run the restart change, Dmitri open the PR, Ava add the Prom alert + extend autoscaler cooldown, Marta create design issue for off-host GC, Ryan we'll add suppression label. Update here when done.

Ben Shah: running now on 10 nodes — patch applied to fleet:gpu-temp1. `for n in $(aws --region us-west1 ec2 describe-instances --filters "Name=tag:fleet,Values=gpu-temp1" ...); do ssh $n 'sed -i "s/JOBS=12/JOBS=1/" /etc/janitor/config && systemctl restart janitor'; done`

Micah Nguyen: thx — seeing attach retries clearing up, nvme await trending down. Will leave CR on for 30m, then revert if stable. :thumbsup:

Ava Chen: PR for alert created: https://git.redwood/ops/alerts/pull/72 — query + 2m for firing.

Dmitri Petrov: PR for janitor config change: https://git.redwood/infra/janitor/pull/518 — includes lockfile logic.

Marta Lopez: created design issue: https://git.redwood/infra/issues/1342 - off-host GC draft.

Micah Nguyen: marking this as resolved for now, will watch through the rest of the rolling restart window. Thanks all. :rocket:

title: #eng-infra: 3301239999
preview:
Ethan Park: Heads-up: USW1 rack-11 showing repeated p95 spikes and a thermal uptick since 01:08. Looks like a single tenant doing aggressive multi-stream prefetch + long decode.

Rina Sato: telemetry matches. NIC bursts and HBM pinned. Which tenant?

Ethan Park: tid-4147 (partner eval). No dedicated carve. InfraBot alerted at 01:10.

InfraBot: ALERT rack-11: gpu_temp=83C, nic_tx=920MB/s, slab_evictions +340%. Ticket INC-10112 created.

Samir Khan: short-term mitigation: soft-fence into noisy-pool + apply egress policer and reduce concurrency. We have knobs: --egress_kbps and --max_streams.

Ava Lopez: please run conservative values first so we don't break the job: egress 650000 kbps, max_streams=2.

Samir Khan: running now:
```
allocatorctl set-tenant-policy --tenant=tid-4147 --pool=noisy-rack-pool --egress_kbps=650000 --max_streams=2
```
InfraBot: ACTION applied.

(45s) InfraBot: policy applied. Monitoring link: https://telemetry.redwood/rack-11/INC-10112

Rina Sato: immediate effect: egress down ~30%, tail p95 improving. KV evictions dropping but still ~1.4x baseline.

Ethan Park: propose automation: if slab_evictions > 250% for 10m post-soft-fence -> schedule fractional carveout + 1hr migration window and notify Billing.

Samir Khan: SGTM. Also logging tid-4147 for post-incident customer advisory: recommend smaller prefetch windows, KV hinting, or short-term reserved slice for sustained throughput.

Ava Lopez: will draft advisory and ops runbook step. Closing thread after 30m stable. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-infra: 2067890123
preview:
Ava Chen: seeing sustained pending queue on dedicated-us-southwest pool, nodes at 92% GPU utilization and pending requests >600, starting to see OOM evictions on some pods :rotating_light:
oncall-bot: ALERT: dedicated-us-southwest GPU pool high-pending-queue (metric_threshold) 2067890125
Ava Chen: autoscaler not catching up, pods launching but CTL reports insufficient quota, suspect kernel panic/slow node recoveries on 3 machines
Ravi Patel: can we shift low-priority orgs to hosted for now? propose routing weight change: hosted 40%, dedicated 60% to relieve q backlog
Marta Lopez: cost question — hosted at 40% for estimated 2h is ~+$1.8k based on current ratecard, ok if we cap to low-priority only
Kai Nguyen: looks reasonable. I'll kick a targeted weight update and enable throttling for embeddings-heavy routes. Doing `curl -X POST "https://control.internal/v1/routing/weights" -H "Authorization: Bearer $SRE_TOKEN" -d '{"region":"us-southwest","hosted_weight":0.4,"priority_filter":"low"}'`
Ava Chen: running now, will monitor queue depth. Also adding temporary prefix-KV cache disable for large TTS jobs to avoid cache churn
oncall-bot: ACTION: routing weights updated by Kai Nguyen at 2067890180 (old:0.85 hosted:0.15 -> new:0.6 dedicated:0.4)
Ravi Patel: confirming low-priority traffic draining: env-gamma and org-plt are switched; watch latency for those orgs in the console
Marta Lopez: please add a kill-switch in case hosted costs spike — `routing:update --rollback 2067890123` suggested
Kai Nguyen: noted and added rollback alias, also set hosted rate-limit 250 rps for low-priority via `control rate set --region us-southwest --tag low --limit 250`
Ava Chen: queue depth down to 320, pending latency median improved from 3.2s to 1.1s on dedicated requests, still monitoring evictions log: `kubectl logs node/gpu-07 | tail -n 40` shows some driver restarts
Ravi Patel: ticketing: creating incident INC-4721 for follow-up, will track node repro and provisioning lag. Assigning to eng-infra and eng-runtime
Marta Lopez: suggest follow-up to capacity planning: add 10% buffer to dedicated pools in southwest during peak hours, and test autoscaler warm-start behavior
Kai Nguyen: creating a canary test to simulate spike and validate weight-switch automation; will push a runbook update to runbook/southwest-gpu-evac.md
Ava Chen: thanks all. We'll keep hosted relay active until node flakiness cleared and autoscaler steady for two cycles. Postmortem later, but immediate goal met — queues falling and user impact limited :thumbsup:

title: #eng-infra: 1976543210
preview:
Priya Rao: Kicking off a small canary for nvidia-driver-580 on the us-west2 GPU pool (5 nodes). Rolling 1 -> 2 -> 5. Watch DKMS builds and NCCL smoke tests. Ill pause if we see >1 failure :rocket:

deploy-bot: Canary rollout started for job `canary/nvidia-driver-580` on `eng-infra-us-west2-gpus`. JobID: canary-1976543210. Targets: node-a,node-b,node-c,node-d,node-e.

Ben Carter: Seeing DKMS build failures on node-b and node-c during the module build step. Pasting snippet: 
```
DKMS: build error for nvidia-580/580.1 (kernel 5.15.0-92-generic)
make[1]: Entering directory '/var/lib/dkms/nvidia-580/build'
ERROR: could not insert 'nvidia': Invalid module format
modpost: ERROR: Unknown symbol in module(s): nvidia_uvm_ioctl
make[1]: *** [modules] Error 1
```

Josh Lee: Which kernel/headers on those nodes? Could be headers mismatch during build.

Ben Carter: uname -r shows 5.15.0-92-generic but linux-headers-5.15.0-90 is installed on both failing nodes (provisioning race).

Sofie Martinez: Ugh, we had a host kernel rollout yesterday in us-west2; looks like some nodes upgraded kernel but headers didn't converge before DKMS triggered.

infra-oncall: Pausing canary in rollout UI and cordoning the remaining un-updated nodes. Marking incident: #INFRA-580-CK. Please collect /var/lib/dkms logs + dmesg.

Ben Carter: Attached additional outputs: 
```
$ modinfo nvidia
filename: /lib/modules/5.15.0-92-generic/extra/nvidia.ko
version: 580.1
srcversion: 1A2B3C4D5E
depends:

$ ls -l /lib/modules/5.15.0-92-generic/extra/
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 123456 Jul 10 10:12 nvidia.ko
```
Also seeing NCCL warnings in a GPU pod on node-c: "NCCL WARN Call to ncclCommInitRank failed: unrecognized symbol" — sounds like mismatch between driver-provided libnccl and container runtime's libnccl.

Priya Rao: Thanks. Actionable summary so far: 1) Pause and rollback canary (done) 2) Confirm kernel/header parity on target nodes 3) Add a preflight that checks `uname -r` vs installed linux-headers package before DKMS runs 4) Add NCCL smoke tests that run after driver install inside a container image matching prod

Josh Lee: For longer term: make driver rollout step idempotent to avoid mid-provision triggers, and ensure kernel upgrades use a two-phase drain so headers and kernels don't diverge. I'll sketch the preflight patch.

Sofie Martinez: Also add a compatibility check matrix for driver-580 vs our common CUDA images (11.8, 12.1). We should automate a sentinel job that mounts the driver and runs a simple all-reduce.

Ben Carter: Note: one node failed to cordon automatically during rollback, had to manually drain because a kube-proxy pod blocked eviction. Filed a kube ticket.

Priya Rao: I'll open a follow-up ticket with the checklist and assign owners: Ben (logs + flaky node), Josh (preflight + rollout change), Sofie (NCCL smoke infra). We'll schedule a re-run after fixes.

infra-oncall: Rollback completed and canary marked failed at 1976546810. Closing canary job canary-1976543210.

Priya Rao: Thanks everyone — great catch. We'll re-run a guarded canary once preflight is in. :white_check_mark:

title: #eng-infra: 1923456789
preview:
Alice Nguyen: Heads-up — we just hit an onboarding blocker for a new customer in eastus2. Need NVv4 / A10G-style quota increase (1-2 nodes to start, target 8 within 2 weeks) + additional public IPs and accelerated networking for the VNET. Opened an Azure case but want to coordinate escalation. Case created: 210123456789.
Ben Carter: Case #? subscription? Also are these dedicated reserved nodes or spot?
Alice Nguyen: case 210123456789 (linked in portal). Subscription ID: xxxxx-aaaa-1111. We need reserved-pref capacity but can accept spot for the initial burst. Main constraint is latency for customers in eastus2, so prefer same region.
Priya Rao: Cost note — NVv4 reserved will be cheaper long-term but we should validate perf vs A100-ish SKUs first. If azure can't supply NVv4 at scale in eastus2, consider euapac fallback or fallback to A100 in nearby region.
Diego Morales: Adding: customer needs private connectivity to their VNet (ExpressRoute) — will need enough public IPs and NAT ports for 8 nodes. Can we ask Azure to provision an isolated quota pool?
Azure Support Bot: New support update for case 210123456789: Initial triage complete. Assigned to engineer. ETA for quota review: 48-72 hours. Reference: azure.support/portal/cases/210123456789
Alice Nguyen: Thanks. If we can push this, ask them whether NVv4 allocation can be reserved for our subscription or if it's per-tenant pool. We had a similar limit where platform-wide availability blocked us.
Maya (Azure CSE): Hi team — I'm Maya, I'll be your CSE. Looking at the case: eastus2 NVv4 capacity is constrained this week. We can submit a capacity request but need: (1) exact VM sku list, (2) expected start/end dates, (3) business justification, (4) VNet/subnet IDs for attachment. Also please confirm if accelerated networking required on boot or can be enabled post-provisioning.
Ben Carter: VM SKUs: Standard_NV8as_v4 x2 initially, ramp to x8 over 14 days. Start date target: 2026-04-01. Business justification: production inference serving for Redwood customer with SLA requirement. Accelerated networking required at boot to pass perf tests.
Alice Nguyen: Provided vNet/subnet in portal. Justification added to case comments. Also asked our AM to loop in platform contacts — putting @Diego in case thread.
Maya (Azure CSE): Thanks — updated case 210123456789. I can elevate to Capacity Team if you mark as "Urgent" and provide a short runbook for failover/alternate region. Otherwise standard quota path ~72 hours. Note: we can offer a temporary "burst" allocation for 48 hours while capacity is arranged, but we need approval from Capacity Team.
Priya Rao: Temporary burst would be perfect to run validation and switch traffic gradually. Diego can you draft the failover plan?
Diego Morales: Yep — will prepare a minimal failover plan: a) DNS weight shift to fallback region, b) temp token-limit on clients, c) autoscaler policy to drain nodes. Posting draft in #eng-oncall by EOD.
Ben Carter: One more ask for the Azure case: can they pre-provision NIC quotas and public IPs as separate quota buckets? In previous tickets they lumped them under one limit which delayed provisioning.
Maya (Azure CSE): I can request separate quota increases for Public IPs and Network Interfaces; please include expected counts. I'll attach a note to the Capacity escalation.
Alice Nguyen: Counts: public IPs 16, NICs 24, NAT ports 1000. Provided in case comments. Also asked for a point-of-contact mobile for rapid SMS updates if needed.
Azure Support Bot: Case update: Capacity escalation submitted by CSE Maya. New internal reference: CAP-2026-0478. Azure will reply with timeline within 24h.
Ben Carter: Awesome. If Azure pushes back on eastus2 capacity, what alternate region should we pick? westus2 or centralus? Priya?
Priya Rao: westus2 has better NVv4 availability historically — but check data residency. If customers require eastus2 only, we'll need the burst plan + temporary fallback to A100 in eu-west to keep latency acceptable.
Alice Nguyen: Noted. For tracking: keeping this thread as the coordination source. I'll post the Azure case updates here as they arrive. :eyes:
Maya (Azure CSE): Quick follow-up — I need the exact subscription contact email (for approval) and a copy of the purchase order or contract line referencing reserved capacity. Please upload to the case so Capacity Team can approve faster.
Diego Morales: Uploading contract snippet now.
Alice Nguyen: Uploaded PO. Marking request as urgent in case comments.
Azure Support Bot: Final update for channel: Capacity Team acknowledged CAP-2026-0478; provisional burst approved for 48h starting 2026-03-25 14:00 UTC. Full provisioning ETA pending; Capacity Team will confirm within next business day.
Ben Carter: Great. Thanks everyone — we'll plan to run smoke tests during the burst window and report results here. :thumbsup:

Ben Carter: Case #? subscription? Also are these dedicated reserved nodes or spot?
Alice Nguyen: case 210123456789 (linked in portal). Subscription ID: xxxxx-aaaa-1111. We need reserved-pref capacity but can accept spot for the initial burst. Main constraint is latency for customers in eastus2, so prefer same region.
Priya Rao: Cost note — NVv4 reserved will be cheaper long-term but we should validate perf vs A100-ish SKUs first. If azure can't supply NVv4 at scale in eastus2, consider euapac fallback or fallback to A100 in nearby region.
Diego Morales: Adding: customer needs private connectivity to their VNet (ExpressRoute) — will need enough public IPs and NAT ports for 8 nodes. Can we ask Azure to provision an isolated quota pool?
Azure Support Bot: New support update for case 210123456789: Initial triage complete. Assigned to engineer. ETA for quota review: 48-72 hours. Reference: azure.support/portal/cases/210123456789
Alice Nguyen: Thanks. If we can push this, ask them whether NVv4 allocation can be reserved for our subscription or if it's per-tenant pool. We had a similar limit where platform-wide availability blocked us.
Maya (Azure CSE): Hi team — I'm Maya, I'll be your CSE. Looking at the case: eastus2 NVv4 capacity is constrained this week. We can submit a capacity request but need: (1) exact VM sku list, (2) expected start/end dates, (3) business justification, (4) VNet/subnet IDs for attachment. Also please confirm if accelerated networking required on boot or can be enabled post-provisioning.
Ben Carter: VM SKUs: Standard_NV8as_v4 x2 initially, ramp to x8 over 14 days. Start date target: 2026-04-01. Business justification: production inference serving for Redwood customer with SLA requirement. Accelerated networking required at boot to pass perf tests.
Alice Nguyen: Provided vNet/subnet in portal. Justification added to case comments. Also asked our AM to loop in platform contacts — putting @Diego in case thread.
Maya (Azure CSE): Thanks — updated case 210123456789. I can elevate to Capacity Team if you mark as "Urgent" and provide a short runbook for failover/alternate region. Otherwise standard quota path ~72 hours. Note: we can offer a temporary "burst" allocation for 48 hours while capacity is arranged, but we need approval from Capacity Team.
Priya Rao: Temporary burst would be perfect to run validation and switch traffic gradually. Diego can you draft the failover plan?
Diego Morales: Yep — will prepare a minimal failover plan: a) DNS weight shift to fallback region, b) temp token-limit on clients, c) autoscaler policy to drain nodes. Posting draft in #eng-oncall by EOD.
Ben Carter: One more ask for the Azure case: can they pre-provision NIC quotas and public IPs as separate quota buckets? In previous tickets they lumped them under one limit which delayed provisioning.
Maya (Azure CSE): I can request separate quota increases for Public IPs and Network Interfaces; please include expected counts. I'll attach a note to the Capacity escalation.
Alice Nguyen: Counts: public IPs 16, NICs 24, NAT ports 1000. Provided in case comments. Also asked for a point-of-contact mobile for rapid SMS updates if needed.
Azure Support Bot: Case update: Capacity escalation submitted by CSE Maya. New internal reference: CAP-2026-0478. Azure will reply with timeline within 24h.
Ben Carter: Awesome. If Azure pushes back on eastus2 capacity, what alternate region should we pick? westus2 or centralus? Priya?
Priya Rao: westus2 has better NVv4 availability historically — but check data residency. If customers require eastus2 only, we'll need the burst plan + temporary fallback to A100 in eu-west to keep latency acceptable.
Alice Nguyen: Noted. For tracking: keeping this thread as the coordination source. I'll post the Azure case updates here as they arrive. :eyes:
Maya (Azure CSE): Quick follow-up — I need the exact subscription contact email (for approval) and a copy of the purchase order or contract line referencing reserved capacity. Please upload to the case so Capacity Team can approve faster.
Diego Morales: Uploading contract snippet now.
Alice Nguyen: Uploaded PO. Marking request as urgent in case comments.
Azure Support Bot: Final update for channel: Capacity Team acknowledged CAP-2026-0478; provisional burst approved for 48h starting 2026-03-25 14:00 UTC. Full provisioning ETA pending; Capacity Team will confirm within next business day.
Ben Carter: Great. Thanks everyone — we'll plan to run smoke tests during the burst window and report results here. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-infra: 1776428891
preview:
Irene Choi: Heads up: we’ve got a driver/CUDA mismatch across the H100 clusters that’s biting the FP8/INT8 benchmark runs.

Irene Choi: Seeing some runs landing on `h100-gke-us-east1b` with NVIDIA driver 535.x, but the benchmark container is `cuda12.4`.

Grace Kim: that explains the failures I hit. got:
```
CUDA error: CUDA driver version is insufficient for CUDA runtime version
```
…only on some nodes.

Isabella Rossi: +1, and even when it doesn’t hard fail, I’m seeing massive variance + first-run spikes that look like JIT/compat behavior.

Vanessa Ortiz: Can you paste `nvidia-smi` from a “bad” node vs “good” node?

Rina Kobayashi: From the runner logs (same git SHA, same args):

Rina Kobayashi:
Bad node:
```
$ nvidia-smi
Driver Version: 535.146.02    CUDA Version: 12.2
GPU  Name        Persistence-M
0    H100 SXM5   On
```

Rina Kobayashi: Good node:
```
Driver Version: 550.54.14     CUDA Version: 12.4
GPU  Name        Persistence-M
0    H100 SXM5   On
```

Keiko Yamamoto: Yep, us-east1b H100 pool is mid-roll on the driver upgrade. Some node groups still pinned to 535 for a different workload.

Irene Choi: For the sprint we need reproducibility, so we can’t have the matrix runner randomly landing on mixed driver fleets.

Rafael Mendes: For canary we can isolate by pool, but for benchmarking it’s pure chaos if the runner doesn’t enforce it.

Neha Kapoor: What’s the fastest mitigation for the next ~48h? Driver upgrades across clusters are risky during the benchmarking window.

Vanessa Ortiz: Short term options:
1) Reroute all H100 benchmark jobs to clusters confirmed on 550.*
2) Pin benchmark container to CUDA 12.2 so it runs on driver 535.*
3) Add a preflight check in the runner to hard-fail if driver < required.

Grace Kim: Option (2) is tricky; FP8 attention path is compiled/tested on 12.4 right now, and we want apples-to-apples with the kernels we’ll ship.

Isabella Rossi: +1. Also we’re collecting Nsight traces; mixing toolchain versions will be messy.

Keiko Yamamoto: Agree. We should keep CUDA 12.4 for H100 FP8 work.

Rina Kobayashi: We already have a runner branch that prints driver/CUDA, but it doesn’t enforce. If we add “min driver for container” gating, we can avoid invalid artifacts in Drive.

Vanessa Ortiz: Proposed immediate plan:
- H100 matrix: run ONLY on pools with driver 550.54+.
- A100 matrix: stay on 535.* with `cuda12.2` image (or whatever the A100 baseline uses today).
- Add preflight: runner checks `nvidia-smi --query-gpu=driver_version --format=csv,noheader` and exits non-zero if not in allowlist.

Irene Choi: I can carve out a dedicated node pool label for the benchmark runner in the “good” H100 clusters and give you the selector.

Rafael Mendes: Please also ensure this doesn’t collide with Dedicated pools. We already had one throughput blip earlier this week.

Keiko Yamamoto: We can add taints + tolerations so only the benchmark namespace can schedule there, and keep it separate from Dedicated node groups.

Neha Kapoor: Do it. Keep it scoped.

Grace Kim: What clusters are “good” right now?

Irene Choi: Current status (as of ~now):
- `h100-gke-us-central1a`: 550.54 ✅
- `h100-gke-us-west1c`: 550.54 ✅
- `h100-gke-us-east1b`: mixed (535/550) ❌ do not use
- `h100-gke-eu-west`: 550.54 ✅ (but capacity tighter)

Vanessa Ortiz: I’ll post a short mapping + recommended runner flags in #eng-runtime too.

Rina Kobayashi: For the preflight check, what do we want: allowlist specific versions or a semver compare?

Keiko Yamamoto: Allowlist is safer for now. We’ve seen weirdness with “550.54.14 vs 550.54.15” formatting across images.

Isabella Rossi: Also capture this in the artifact metadata (driver + container tag) so we can filter if anything slips through.

FleetBot: FYI: Cluster `h100-gke-us-east1b` nodepool `np-h100-sxm5-spot` is currently at 62% nodes on driver 535.146.02 (rollout in progress). :warning:

Irene Choi: Ok action items:
- (Irene) Create benchmark-only H100 nodepool selector/taint on `us-central1a` + `us-west1c` and share with Rina/Isabella.
- (Rina) Update matrix runner to *fail fast* on driver mismatch + print a clear error.
- (Vanessa) Update the benchmark runbook/checklist to say “no us-east1b until rollout completes” + list required versions.
- (Keiko) Confirm Dedicated pools are isolated from these node groups.

Rafael Mendes: + add a note for canary: if we see perf drift by region, first check driver version before blaming kernels.

Grace Kim: :thumbsup: thanks. rerunning the failed H100 cases now on us-central.

Neha Kapoor: Ping me if you hit a hard capacity wall on the “good” pools; I’ll help unblock additional hours, but keep the guardrails.

Vanessa Ortiz: I’ll also ask Observability to add driver_version as a tag on the benchmark dashboards if it’s not already there. Would make this visible earlier next time.

Irene Choi: Cool. I’ll update once the us-east rollout is complete, but treat it as off-limits for the sprint unless we can guarantee 100% 550+.

title: #eng-infra: 1770669135
preview:
Peter Holtz: seeing burn-rate alerts come in more frequently on Dedicated during burst (esp EU). can we align on what "capacity mitigation" means in practice? like, do we have an explicit headroom target per pool + when do we page Fleet Ops vs just traffic-shape?

Aisha Bello: +1. from SRE side we can do routing knobs, but we need a clear contract from fleet: "if Dedicated tier is burning, we protect it first" and what actions are allowed (borrow capacity? preempt hosted?)

Neha Kapoor: yep, we need to be explicit. Dedicated is SLA-bearing, Hosted is best-effort. If we’re in a budget burn state for Dedicated, infra posture should be: (1) preserve dedicated p95/p99, (2) shed/route hosted elsewhere before we violate dedicated.

Miles Thornton: can we frame this as a runbook section with triggers? e.g. burn rate > 2x for 30m OR p95 latency breach in a region => "capacity event".

Rafael Mendes: also, to be clear: routing can do "hosted fallback model" and "cross-region" but capacity is the limiting factor. we keep getting stuck in "we’ll add GPUs" but then it’s 4 hrs later.

Fleet Ops (oncall): from fleet side: we can move nodes between pools in some cases, but it’s not instant. fastest levers are: enable extra warm capacity already provisioned, lift max autoscale, and pause non-prod / batch.

Peter Holtz: what’s a sane headroom target we can commit to? we tossed around 10% but that feels too tight for spikes.

Neha Kapoor: dedicated pools should have a higher headroom than hosted. i’d like 20% target headroom (available slots vs provisioned) in-region for Dedicated, measured during peak hours.

Thomas Baird: careful: how are we defining headroom? tokens/sec capacity is variable w/ model mix + context length. should we define it as "concurrency headroom" at the routing admission layer (pre-kv/cache), or GPU idle?

Miles Thornton: agree. "GPU idle" is misleading. we should define headroom as "schedulable capacity above p95 observed load" per pool using our admission controller metrics.

Logan Wright: telemetry can expose a "pool_capacity_effective" gauge (derived) and "pool_load". we can put a recording rule: headroom = (capacity - load)/capacity.

Sergio Costa: for dashboards: we can do per-region per-pool headroom + annotate when we enter SLO burn state. already drafting it for the tiered SLO views.

Mina Haddad: from routing side: if Dedicated headroom < X for Y minutes, we can automatically clamp Hosted traffic for that region (raise admission price / shed at gateway) + increase cross-region routing for hosted.

Aisha Bello: question: do we allow Dedicated to "borrow" from Hosted GPU pool, or do we keep hard separation?

Fleet Ops (oncall): we can do temporary borrowing IF hardware is compatible + images match. but it’s operationally risky and not something we want to do every incident.

Neha Kapoor: policy: Dedicated gets priority, but we shouldn’t rely on manual pool shuffles as the default. baseline should be maintaining the headroom so we don’t need to borrow.

Peter Holtz: ok, so: Dedicated headroom target 20% (effective capacity) and Hosted 10%? is that what we want to publish?

Neha Kapoor: Hosted can be lower because we can degrade (fallback models, shed). i’m ok with 10% target for Hosted, but with an explicit "may go to 0 in incident".

Rafael Mendes: can we put a hard trigger for paging Fleet Ops? we need an escalation path that’s not "ask in slack".

Aisha Bello: proposing: page Fleet Ops when (a) Dedicated burn-rate alert at 10x/1h fires OR (b) Dedicated headroom < 10% for 15m AND p95 latency above SLO.

Fleet Ops (oncall): paging criteria works. also add (c) allocator queue depth > threshold for >5m (we see that before latency goes red).

Thomas Baird: please add in the doc that pool_headroom is computed at 1m resolution, 5m rollup, and that we ignore known metric gaps during ingest backpressure.

Logan Wright: noted. we can add a data-quality flag: if coverage < 99% for the last 15m, headroom alert is "informational" not paging.

Mina Haddad: if we’re protecting Dedicated first, what about a scenario where a single Dedicated customer is the spike driver? do we have per-account caps or do we just eat it?

Neha Kapoor: Dedicated contracts have committed throughput, and burst is allowed within reason. if one account is saturating, we first enforce their agreed limits (rate limit / admission control) before impacting others.

Peter Holtz: so the operational order is:
1) confirm it’s real (no metric gap)
2) route/shed Hosted in-region
3) enforce Dedicated customer caps if exceeding commit
4) add warm capacity / lift autoscale in Dedicated pool
5) if still red, borrow from Hosted pool as last resort

Aisha Bello: yes. plus: if Dedicated budget is already "red" (exhaustion), we should freeze non-emergency deploys that touch routing/gateway + pause model rollouts. (ties to error budget policy)

FleetOps Bot: FleetOps Bot pinned a message: "Capacity Mitigation Guidelines (draft): https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INFRA/Capacity+Mitigation+Guidelines+for+SLO+Events"

Sergio Costa: link looks right. i’ll add a section "headroom definitions" + the paging triggers Aisha listed.

Miles Thornton: also need explicit "regionalization": if EU-West dedicated is red and US-East has spare, we do NOT shift Dedicated cross-region unless customer opted in. Hosted can move; Dedicated default is stay-in-region.

Mina Haddad: routing can enforce that: Dedicated policy = region pin unless override flag in account config.

Rafael Mendes: can we document "who is IC" for capacity events? right now it’s ambiguous when SRE pages fleet.

Neha Kapoor: if it’s SLO burn, SRE is IC. Fleet Ops is "Operations" role. Fleet Ops owns executing capacity changes + communicating ETAs back to IC.

Fleet Ops (oncall): works. we’ll add a small template for updates: "action taken / expected impact / ETA / risks".

Thomas Baird: ask: are we ok with publishing "20% headroom" as a target publicly? (internally fine, but customer-facing maybe not.)

Neha Kapoor: internal only. customer-facing is SLA/SLO, not our headroom. but we need this internally to make SLOs real.

Aisha Bello: next steps: Sergio update the guidelines doc + dashboards; Logan add the headroom recording rule; Fleet Ops confirm paging route; Mina add "hosted clamp" automation when dedicated headroom is low.

Peter Holtz: +1. i’ll open a short ticket to add "capacity event" section to the SLO breach mitigation runbook with the exact triggers + ordering.

Fleet Ops (oncall): please also add which GPU pools are "movable" vs "pinned" (some of the H100s are pre-allocated for specific dedicated clusters).

Sergio Costa: ack. adding a table per region with pool constraints + owners.

Neha Kapoor: thanks all. let’s treat this as the baseline for Q1: Dedicated headroom 20% target, paging triggers defined, and Hosted explicitly degradable. if we can’t hold 20% consistently, we need to revisit capacity commitments with PM Dedicated.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: i’ll sync with Lauren/Nikhil after this lands so Product doesn’t overpromise.

Peter Holtz: closing loop: please drop any edits in the pinned doc. i’ll check back EOD to make sure we have final wording for the escalation criteria.

title: #eng-infra: 1768640205
preview:
Victor Alvarez: during/after the controlled failover window, can folks sanity check cloud provider status + any obvious network symptoms? reachability signal is flipping `us-east -> impaired` from eu-west probes but provider status looks green at a glance. want to make sure we’re not chasing ghosts.

Emily Zhao: looking now. which provider view are we using for prod? AWS PHD + public status page?

Victor Alvarez: yep, AWS. regions in play are us-east-1 + eu-west-1. this was right after traffic shift.

Irene Choi: do we have a timestamp range? trying to correlate to our edge NAT / transit alarms.

Victor Alvarez: roughly 10:12–10:24 PT (during the redirect). after 10:24 it looks mostly recovered but probes still noisy.

Allison Grant: FYI reachability panel in Failover Signals dashboard is showing 2–4% failed probes EU->US and elevated p95 connect time. errors aren’t huge but it tripped the “corroboration” gate.

Sergio Costa: +1, I see probe dimension `source_region=eu-west target_region=us-east` is the only one spiking. US->EU looks clean.

Emily Zhao: AWS public status page: no open issues for us-east-1 or eu-west-1.
Emily Zhao: PHD (personal health dashboard) also nothing posted in our acct (at least on my view). doesn’t mean no networking flap though.

Victor Alvarez: can you check if there were any `Network Performance` events / impaired AZ notices? (sometimes shows as “informational”).

Emily Zhao: digging. nothing yet. closest is “elevated API error rates” in a different region earlier today.

Miles Thornton: if this is asymmetric (EU->US only), it could be transit provider pathing or a single edge POP. do we have traceroute/mtr from the perf-canary nodes?

Victor Alvarez: running now from `canary-euw1-03`.

Victor Alvarez: 
```bash
mtr -r -c 50 api.us-east.prod.redwood.ai
```

Vanessa Ortiz: also check if us-east was tight on headroom during the drain. if we were shedding/accepting fewer conns, could look like “unreachable” depending on probe behavior.

Peter Holtz: gateway logs show some spikes in `connect_timeout` and `upstream_reset` during that window, but it’s not pure 503 shedding. looks more like SYN retries / handshake slow.

Victor Alvarez: output (sanitized):
```text
HOST: canary-euw1-03                       Loss%   Snt   Last   Avg  Best  Wrst StDev
  1. 100.64.x.x                             0.0%    50    0.4   0.5   0.3   1.2   0.2
  2. ae12-0.lon1-border (provider)          0.0%    50    1.1   1.3   0.9  10.2   1.3
  3. (redacted transit)                     2.0%    50   88.5  92.1  82.9 160.4  14.7
  4. * * *
  5. us-east-edge (provider)                3.0%    50   95.1 101.4  86.7 210.3  28.9
  6. api.us-east.prod.redwood.ai            2.0%    50   97.8 105.0  90.2 230.7  31.1
```
seems like mild loss + jitter on the transit hop, and a few timeouts.

Irene Choi: that aligns w/ what I’m seeing: our VPC flow logs from euw1 NAT GW show elevated `REJECT` due to `SYN_SENT` aging out (timeouts), not application rejects.

Keiko Yamamoto: if probes are TCP connect + small request, they’ll be sensitive to handshake + retrans. any chance we hit conntrack limits on EU side?

Irene Choi: checked. conntrack not near limit on euw1 egress; also no big spike in new connections overall.

Rafael Mendes: do we have any other independent signal? like ping mesh / cross-region ICMP? (even if blocked, maybe we have internal mesh).

Victor Alvarez: ICMP is mostly blocked on the public endpoint, but we have the perf-canary “connect + TLS handshake” metric. it’s the one elevated.

Allison Grant: yep. `probe_tls_handshake_seconds{source=eu-west,target=us-east}` p95 jumped from ~120ms to ~900ms for ~8–10 mins. failure rate peaked at 4.2%.

Miles Thornton: that’s enough to count as real impairment for reachability, even if AWS didn’t publish. question: did we *need* to fail over based on that alone, or did it just contribute?

Victor Alvarez: it wasn’t the sole trigger. controller required reachability + either 5xx burn OR tail latency burn. we got reachability + tail latency burn in us-east (client-observed p99).

Peter Holtz: client tail latency makes sense if handshake slow. retries would amplify too.

Sergio Costa: do we have BGP/anycast anomalies? (I know our anycast is limited, but DNS steer might’ve changed pathing.)

Victor Alvarez: DNS TTL drop went out yesterday (per the terraform PR). could be more clients re-resolving and landing on different paths. but this is probes (not clients), so less likely.

Emily Zhao: FYI I checked AWS “Global Network” dashboard (the internal one). it shows “increased latency” between EU and US around that time but no incident ID. screenshot in #eng-sre already? I can post here if useful.

Irene Choi: please post.

Emily Zhao: uploading now… (screenshot: EU-WEST->US-EAST latency band elevated, no red status).

Rafael Mendes: that’s helpful. suggests provider-level symptom without formal incident.

Vanessa Ortiz: from capacity side, us-east had ~28% headroom at time of trigger; eu-west had ~35%. so not a capacity exhaustion artifact.

Keiko Yamamoto: could still be local endpoint overload causing handshake delays (SYN backlog). do we see SYN cookies / listen queue overruns on the ALB/NLB?

Peter Holtz: NLB metrics: `TCP_Target_Reset_Count` modest bump but not huge. `NewFlowCount` stable. feels network-y.

Victor Alvarez: agreed. also mtr shows loss before hitting our edge.

Miles Thornton: action item: in game day checklist, add “snapshot provider network view” + “capture mtr from canary nodes” as required evidence when reachability flips. helps tune corroboration without hand-waving.

Allison Grant: +1. also, our reachability alert currently pages at 2% failed probes over 5m. might want a longer hold-down / require sustained p95 handshake > X ms (not just failures).

Sergio Costa: careful: longer windows slow detection for real blackholes. maybe do dual-threshold: fast trip at high failure (>=15%), slower corroboration at low failure + high latency.

Victor Alvarez: makes sense. for today’s event, it was “low fail + high latency” category.

Irene Choi: also, we should tag these as “provider symptom suspected” in audit events? at least a freeform note field. enterprise asks “what happened” and we need something better than “reachability bad”.

Rafael Mendes: we can add a runbook step: “check AWS status/PHD + internal global network dashboard” and link it in the incident-bot summary.

Emily Zhao: fwiw, symptoms look resolved now. re-running mtr from euw1 is clean: 0% loss, avg ~92ms, no timeouts.

Victor Alvarez: great. that validates reachability signal wasn’t totally bogus. it caught a transient transit issue.

Peter Holtz: gateway connect timeouts back to baseline as well.

Miles Thornton: can we drop this into the post-gameday notes? especially the asymmetry + no-PHD-event detail.

Victor Alvarez: yep, I’ll paste summary into the retro doc + link this thread.

Irene Choi: thanks all. :thumbsup: (reaction :thumbsup: from Irene Choi)

Allison Grant: I’ll open a small follow-up for alert tuning + evidence capture fields (Sergio/Miles I’ll loop you).

title: #eng-infra: 1768469814
preview:
Victor Alvarez: heads up, we’re proposing DNS TTL reductions for regional failover (Hosted + some Dedicated endpoints). Want a quick risk assessment here before we merge the tf changes.

Victor Alvarez: current state (most public records): TTL=60s (a few are 300s still). Proposal: drop to 20s for the failover-weighted records, keep everything else at 60s.

Irene Choi: 20s is… aggressive but maybe fine. Can you clarify which records exactly? `api.redwoodinference.com` is behind the geo/latency policy right?

Victor Alvarez: yep. Specifically:
- `api.redwoodinference.com` (latency/weighted policy)
- `stream.api.redwoodinference.com`
- `dedicated.<region>.redwoodinference.com` NOT in scope (those are customer-specific and already pinned)
- a couple internal healthcheck CNAMEs used by probes

Miles Thornton: +1 to being careful. Remember TTL is only one lever; resolvers don’t always respect low TTL.

Emily Zhao: confirming: some enterprise customers terminate behind corporate resolvers that clamp to min TTL (I’ve seen 30s/60s). Also Java apps tend to cache DNS “forever” unless configured (security property). So lower TTL helps, but it’s not deterministic.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE side: we’re trying to get faster convergence during a region impairment, but we don’t want surprise traffic shifts during normal ops. If TTL change causes more query load or weird caching behavior, that’s a bad trade.

Victor Alvarez: risks I’m tracking:
1) Resolver TTL floors (min TTL) => won’t see benefit for some clients
2) Negative caching (NXDOMAIN) if we misconfigure or a provider returns transient NXDOMAIN
3) Increased QPS to authoritative (Route53) and potential throttling (unlikely but worth quantifying)
4) Partial propagation mismatches during change window => weird split-brain (some clients re-resolve, others don’t)

Allison Grant: observability angle: if we do this, we should annotate dashboards and watch:
- DNS query rate at Route53
- `5xx` + tail latency by region
- ingress RPS shifts (esp. eu-west)
If there’s a subtle traffic drift we need to detect it fast.

Peter Holtz: also watch retries. Lower TTL can cause more reconnect churn on clients that resolve per request, but main risk is *failback* when a region recovers. If the record flips back quickly, some clients will thrash. We should pair with hold-down timers in routing policy.

Miles Thornton: right, DNS TTL != policy flap protection. The controller has hold-down, but DNS-based steering has to respect that too (don’t “bounce” answers).

Irene Choi: provider caveats:
- Some recursive resolvers serve stale records longer under load/outage (serve-stale RFC). During an internet event, low TTL might not matter.
- Cloudflare / public resolvers sometimes prefetch and extend caching behavior.
- On AWS, Route53 health checks can lag; but we’re not relying solely on R53 health checks for failover anyway.

Victor Alvarez: yep, this TTL change is meant to reduce the *best-case* convergence time when routing weights are adjusted, not to be the only trigger.

Emily Zhao: question: are we changing TTL for apex/ALIAS? If it’s ALIAS to a load balancer, TTL semantics are a bit weird. Route53 sets TTL but ALIAS answers can behave differently than plain A/AAAA from a client POV.

Victor Alvarez: `api.*` is ALIAS to regional NLBs via latency-based routing. TTL should still apply, but yes: some resolvers treat ALIAS just like A records. We’ll test with `dig` against common resolvers.

Rafael Mendes: can we do staged rollout? like: lower TTL only for a canary hostname first, run a planned weight flip in staging, then do prod.

Victor Alvarez: rollout plan draft:
Phase 0 (today): add canary record `api-canary.redwoodinference.com` with TTL=20s, same routing policy as `api.*`.
Phase 1 (staging): run a controlled weight shift us-east -> eu-west and measure convergence. Record:
- time-to-traffic-shift at edge (ingress)
- DNS query rates
- client error/latency
Phase 2 (prod, low blast): change TTL to 30s first on `stream.api.*` only (lower traffic), observe 24h
Phase 3 (prod): change TTL to 20s on `api.*` during a weekday window w/ oncall present
Rollback: revert TTL to 60s + stop further changes if:
- Route53 qps spikes >2x baseline for >15m
- unexplained region traffic shift >10% sustained

Sean Gallagher: I like staged. But I’d prefer 30s as final unless we can prove 20s doesn’t create noise. The bigger win might be getting rid of the 300s outliers.

Irene Choi: +1. Removing 300s is low-risk / high value.

Victor Alvarez: fair. Alternative proposal:
- Standardize to 60s everywhere
- Drop only failover steering records to 30s (not 20s)
- Keep canary at 20s for measurement

Miles Thornton: works for me. Also check: some mobile clients use OS caching with min TTL (iOS) + captive networks. Don’t assume perfect.

Allison Grant: if we do canary, please ensure dashboards include: `dns_ttl_seconds` label or annotation so we can correlate.

Victor Alvarez: we can add an annotation in the failover dashboard + a runbook note. I can also emit a one-time “infra change” event to the timeline feed.

Peter Holtz: any interaction with anycast? we have anycast notes in the networking doc but our public endpoint is still DNS->regional NLB. Anycast is only for internal edge proxies in a couple POPs, right?

Victor Alvarez: correct. No anycast change here.

Emily Zhao: another caveat: if we lower TTL but keep connection reuse high (HTTP/2), many clients won’t re-resolve until reconnect. So the effective traffic shift time is bounded by connection lifetimes too.

Miles Thornton: which is why the gateway redirect + retry-after stuff matters. DNS is just one piece.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Victor: update tf PR to set TTL=30s for prod records, add canary at 20s
- Allison: prep dashboard annotation + quick query for Route53 QPS
- Miles/Victor: add note to game day plan re: DNS TTL expectations (not deterministic)

Terraform Bot: PR opened: redwood-terraform/pr-1442-dns-ttl-and-failover-routing-updates (diff includes TTL changes for `api.redwoodinference.com` + new `api-canary.redwoodinference.com`).

Irene Choi: can you also add a guardrail in the PR description: “no other routing policy changes included” to keep scope tight.

Victor Alvarez: yep, will do. Also adding a checklist:
- [ ] verify no 300s TTL remain on failover records
- [ ] verify healthcheck CNAMEs unchanged
- [ ] confirm Route53 limits + current qps baseline

Sean Gallagher: and include explicit “stop” conditions + who has rollback authority (infra oncall + SRE lead). We don’t want to debate mid-change.

Victor Alvarez: ack. Rollback authority: infra oncall (primary) with SRE lead consulted, Irene as final escalation.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: once PR is in, I’ll add the dashboard annotations & a temporary alert on Route53 qps anomalies.

Miles Thornton: one more thing: make sure we don’t reduce TTL on any records that are used for data residency pinning (some enterprise tenants depend on region-locked hostnames).

Victor Alvarez: confirmed out of scope. Only the global Hosted API hostnames.

Irene Choi: ok. +1 to proceed with the 30s plan + canary at 20s. Let’s schedule staging measurement before prod change.

Victor Alvarez: sounds good. I’ll update PR + drop a calendar hold for staging test with Emily + Allison.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. linking this thread in the reliability initiative doc so it doesn’t get lost.

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. linking this thread in the reliability initiative doc so it doesn’t get lost.

title: #eng-infra: 1765123456
preview:
Ava Chen: quick ask — can we add a heatmap that highlights GPU slot sparsity across racks and regions? seeing weird cold pockets in eu-west.
Tom Briggs: yup, we should do a heatmap + a sparklines row. want it to surface both utilization and "sparsity" (slots idle > 20% for > 30m)
Ava Chen: exactly. aim is to correlate sparsity with thermal deltas and cost (warm-slotting wasted cycles)
Priya Nair: FYI we also need to treat eu-west with a different SLO — smaller fleet and tighter latency reqs. alert thresholds can't be same as us-west.
Jorge Ruiz: re: query — here's a starting promql I used locally:
```
sum by (rack, region) (rate(gpu_utilization_seconds_total[5m])) / sum by (rack, region) (machine_cpu_cores)
```
but better to use node-level allocatable vram and nonzero allocations: 
```
(sum by(rack,region)(gpu_allocated_vram_bytes) / sum by(rack,region)(gpu_total_vram_bytes))
```
Tom Briggs: for sparsity detection I prefer a count of slots with <5% utilization over 30m:
```
count_over_time((gpu_slot_utilization < 0.05)[30m:30s])
```
then divide by count of slots per rack for a ratio.
grafana-bot: snapshot created: https://grafana.internal/snapshot/abcd1234 (expires in 7d)
Ava Chen: nice. can we add an annotation stream for scheduled maintenance and autoscaler events? those explain sudden cold pockets.
Tom Briggs: yes — add prom label from k8s_events or autoscaler events. panel thresholds:
- yellow: sparsity_ratio > 0.2
- red: sparsity_ratio > 0.4
Priya Nair: also add thermal-delta overlay: rack_inlet_temp 90p - 10p > 5C => indicates uneven cooling causing hot spots
Jorge Ruiz: for SLO tuning: different alert rules per region, e.g., eu-west p95 latency alert at 250ms, us-west at 300ms. attach region label to alertmanager route.
Ava Chen: cost note — if warm-slotting reduces cold pockets but increases reserved hours, we need a cost dashboard; link to billing query?
Tom Briggs: add a cost panel using `sum(cost_per_gpu_hour * gpu_hours_by_region)` and correlate with sparsity_ratio.
Priya Nair: propose a recording rule for sparsity_ratio per rack to reduce query load:
```
- record: infra:gpu_sparsity_ratio:avg
  expr: sum(count_over_time((gpu_slot_utilization < 0.05)[30m:1m])) by (rack,region) / sum(count_over_time((gpu_slot_utilization >= 0)[30m:1m])) by (rack,region)
```
Jorge Ruiz: agree. also debounce alerts by 15m to avoid flapping during deploys.
Ava Chen: what's the rollout plan for the new panels? small canary to prod grafana org?
Tom Briggs: create new folder `Ops/GPU Heatmaps` -> start with 2 view panels (sparsity heatmap, thermal delta overlay) in canary org. PR the dashboard json to infra/grafana repo.
grafana-bot: grafana PR created: https://git.internal/infra/grafana/pulls/432
Priya Nair: tagging on-call: @tom @ava — can we get a follow-up runbook entry for interpreting sparsity vs preemption?
Tom Briggs: will draft runbook. action items:
- Tom: PR dashboard + recording rule (due EOD Thu)
- Jorge: verify alertmanager route and debounce (due Fri)
- Priya: add cost panel wiring and billing query (due Fri)
- Ava: annotate maintenance windows for last 30d (due Tue)
Ava Chen: thanks all :thumbsup:
Tom Briggs: +1
grafana-bot: deployment of dashboard to canary completed at 1765123620

title: #eng-infra: 1762643912
preview:
Irene Choi: heads up, for hot-route protection we need to settle which fleet signals are actually reliable for admission control triggers. seeing debate in reviews: GPU saturation vs queue depth vs scheduler lag. want to lock a recommendation this week + note region-specific thresholds.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. GPU % is the one everyone reaches for but it lies a lot in practice (esp in us-east). queue depth seems closer to “customer pain” but also depends on batching settings.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE view: the main thing is signals that correlate with p95/p99 latency BEFORE we’re on fire. GPU util tends to peg at 95% during healthy steady state for some pools.

Keiko Yamamoto: also autoscaler can mask things. gpu-util can be high while we’re scaling out, so if we shed based on util we’d shed too aggressively.

Jamal Pierce: i pulled a quick snapshot from last week burst (us-east) — GPU util was 92–97% for ~40 mins, but latency only spiked for chat when queue depth crossed a threshold.

Jamal Pierce: excerpt (from notes):
- us-east chat pool baseline gpu_util: ~88–93% at normal daytime
- eu-west baseline gpu_util: ~70–82%
- queue_depth_req (avg) baseline: ~0.2–0.8
- when queue_depth_req sustained > 6, p95 latency started climbing fast

Robert King: so maybe gpu util isn’t a trigger, but still useful as context / to validate we’re not just in a metric bug.

Elena Popov: routing needs something stable and low-cardinality. per-GPU metrics are expensive in decision path; aggregated per-pool / per-cluster is fine. queue depth per route-group would be ideal.

Julian Moore: runtime side: scheduler lag is probably the cleanest leading indicator. We now export `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95` + `runtime.queue_depth_requests` (post PR-27435). Both are already aggregated by pool.

Logan Wright: careful on tags. we had the cardinality incident last month when route_id slipped into overload metrics :grimacing: if routing wants per-route-group, we can do `route_group` not per-tenant.

Aisha Bello: +1. The alerting side also prefers a composite: (queue depth high) AND (sched lag high) for N minutes.

Irene Choi: what about GPU saturation as a guardrail though? e.g. if queue depth low but GPU is pegged, that’s still a sign we’re out of headroom? or could just be long sequences / kernels.

Sofia Ivanova: queue depth low + gpu pegged is often long prompts with continuous batching. scheduler is “busy” but not necessarily behind. it only becomes a problem if lag grows (scheduler can’t keep up) or queue accumulates.

Vanessa Ortiz: also MIG fragmentation can make util look high with less effective throughput. we’ve seen util ~95% even when one slice is starved.

Peter Holtz: for circuit breakers we usually look at:
1) scheduler lag p95 (service time backlog)
2) pending queue depth (arrival > service)
3) 5xx spike (already failing)
GPU util is mostly a “capacity planning” indicator not “admission now” indicator.

Robert King: question: do we have consistent queue depth across regions? i remember eu-west uses a slightly different batching config.

Julian Moore: yes but magnitude differs. queue depth is “requests waiting to be admitted to scheduler” so it depends on front-door concurrency + batch size.

Jamal Pierce: that’s where region-specific thresholds come in. eu-west queue depth baseline is lower; using a global threshold of 6 might be too high (we’d react late). maybe use percentile over baseline per region/pool.

Aisha Bello: +100. absolute thresholds are brittle. can we do: trigger when `queue_depth` > max(absolute_min, baseline_p95 * factor)?

Keiko Yamamoto: baseline computation in routing sounds risky for data plane complexity. but we can precompute per-region thresholds in config (control plane) and ship them.

Elena Popov: yep, we can keep routing simple:
- read per-route-group budget
- read per-region “pressure thresholds” for pool
- evaluate with last N seconds aggregated metrics from fleet signals service
No dynamic baseline calc in routing.

Irene Choi: can infra own the per-region threshold table (at least initial values) and we can revisit after canary?

Vanessa Ortiz: yes. propose:
Signal A (primary): scheduler_lag_ms_p95
Signal B (secondary): queue_depth_requests_avg (or p95)
Signal C (context only): gpu_util_p95

Vanessa Ortiz: then define pressure states per region/pool:
- WARN: lag > X OR qdepth > Y for 2m
- HOT: lag > X2 AND qdepth > Y2 for 1m
- CRIT: lag > X3 AND qdepth > Y3 for 30s (activate strict admission)

Rafael Mendes: this maps nicely to “start shedding best-effort at HOT, protect enterprise budgets at CRIT”.

Logan Wright: for dashboards, please standardize names:
- `fleet.pressure_state{region,pool}` as a gauge (0/1/2/3)
- `overload.shed_rate{region,route_group,tier,reason}`
We can annotate when pressure_state flips.

Peter Holtz: note: pressure_state should be derived in control plane / fleet signals service, not computed differently by each component.

Robert King: do we have an agreed metric source? i’ve seen both prometheus and the fleet-signals gRPC.

Vanessa Ortiz: for this project: fleet-signals gRPC (aggregated) is the source of truth. prom is for debugging.

Jamal Pierce: region notes (tentative, based on last 14d):
- us-east (chat pool): WARN lag>120ms or qdepth>4; HOT lag>250ms + qdepth>8; CRIT lag>400ms + qdepth>12
- eu-west (chat pool): WARN lag>90ms or qdepth>3; HOT lag>200ms + qdepth>6; CRIT lag>320ms + qdepth>9
These need validation during game day.

Aisha Bello: add a “don’t flap” rule please. if we enter HOT/CRIT require 60s below WARN to exit.

Keiko Yamamoto: also check autoscaling interaction: when scale-out in progress, lag can oscillate. maybe incorporate `pending_scale_actions` as a dampener? (not a trigger)

Sofia Ivanova: be careful: when scale-out happens, new replicas warm KV cache etc. you still might want to shed briefly to protect tail.

Irene Choi: ok, decision direction:
- use scheduler lag + queue depth for triggers
- GPU util is contextual only
- thresholds per region/pool in config, owned by infra + tuned via canary/game day

Irene Choi: action items:
1) @Jamal Pierce share the threshold table draft in the rollout doc + include “how derived”
2) @Vanessa Ortiz + @Robert King confirm fleet-signals gRPC fields + update contract doc
3) @Elena Popov ensure routing consumes pressure_state + doesn’t query raw gpu util
4) @Logan Wright align dashboard queries + make sure tags are bounded (region/pool/route_group only)

Vanessa Ortiz: ack. i’ll post the gRPC fields here once i confirm final names.

Julian Moore: current names from runtime exporter (double-check):
- `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95{region,pool}`
- `runtime.queue_depth_requests_avg{region,pool}`
- `runtime.gpu_utilization_p95{region,pool}`
Fleet-signals service already aggregates to `fleet.*` equivalents.

Rafael Mendes: once we have the table, we’ll run it in the game day and see if we start shedding at the right time (before enterprise p95 blows past SLO). :thumbsup:

Irene Choi: cool. please keep this thread updated with any “gotchas” discovered in eu-west; we’ve been burned by copying us-east thresholds before.

title: #eng-infra: 1760710033
preview:
Sahana Iyer: heads up: the signature verification integration tests are now the slowest step in `private-installer` CI 😬

Sahana Iyer: `bundle-verify-integration` job went from ~6m to ~28-35m after we added tamper/replay/downgrade cases. We need to get this back under ~10m or we’re gonna start timing out in release train.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118244 for redwood-private-installer / main failed
ci-bot: Step `bundle-verify-integration` exceeded soft timeout (30m). Log: https://buildkite.example.redwood/...

Devon Price: looking at the logs now. a lot of time is `sha256sum` over big fixtures + re-generating offline bundle each test case.

Ethan Park: yeah, each test currently:
1) builds bundle (tar + generate manifest)
2) signs manifest
3) unpacks
4) modifies artifact
5) runs preflight

Ethan Park: so we’re doing the expensive steps N times.

Jordan Lee: manifest canonicalization step is also re-running every time. not huge but it adds up.

Sahana Iyer: can we make a “golden” bundle fixture once per job and then derive tampered variants cheaply?

Devon Price: +1. also the fixture includes a big image tarball (we added it to simulate offline image import). hashing that is killing us.

Connor O'Brien: release side: we don’t actually need a 3GB image tar to validate signature semantics. we need to validate that we *would* hash the referenced artifact, but the artifact can be small in tests.

Marcus Lin: from security POV, as long as the test still exercises:
- content hash validation
- signature verification
- fail-closed behavior
- downgrade protection
we can shrink the bytes.

Sean Gallagher: also worth splitting “fast unit-ish verification tests” vs “true end-to-end on a full bundle”. run the full bundle one nightly?

Ethan Park: we already have unit tests for canonicalization + envelope parsing. the integration suite is meant to be “bundle on disk + preflight CLI”.

Ethan Park: but we could do:
- one e2e uses a “medium” bundle (still realistic layout)
- tamper variants derived from that bundle via file copy + single edit

Sahana Iyer: what’s the slowest operation by flame graph / rough breakdown?

Devon Price: rough from build logs:
- `create_bundle()` ~9-12m (mostly tar + sha256s)
- `verify_bundle()` ~30-60s each
- repeated 12 times = pain

Devon Price: also `docker save` in one fixture is ~4m. why are we doing docker save here?

Ethan Park: legacy from older offline install tests. for signing, we only need to test that the manifest lists artifacts + hashes and we verify them offline.

Jordan Lee: can we replace docker save fixture with just a dummy file named `images/redwood-runtime.tar` that is small?

Marcus Lin: yes, but keep the path + semantics. the test should ensure that *changing that file* triggers hash mismatch.

Sahana Iyer: ok. proposal:
1) replace heavy image fixture with small deterministic tar (like `tar --mtime=0 --sort=name ...`)
2) generate base bundle once, cache it across test cases
3) for each test: copy base bundle dir (hardlink?) and mutate only one file

Sahana Iyer: for (2) we can do `TestMain` / shared setup if in Go.

Ethan Park: suite is in Go. we can use `sync.Once` and write base bundle into `$TMPDIR/base_bundle`.

Devon Price: copying the bundle dir might still be expensive. can we do copy-on-write? linux hardlinks via `cp -al`?

Sahana Iyer: Buildkite agents are linux, so `cp -al` should work. but careful: mutating file should break hardlink (need to copy the file you mutate, not write in-place).

Jordan Lee: for tamper cases we usually modify manifest or one artifact. we can do:
- copy tree as hardlinks
- for the file to modify, `cp` it first to break link, then edit

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Connor O'Brien: also add `BUILDKITE_CACHE`/artifact caching? we could upload the base bundle as build artifact for the step and have other steps consume it.

Sahana Iyer: within a single step we can probably avoid cross-step caching. cross-build caching is tricky (branch isolation) but possible.

Devon Price: simplest win is: stop regenerating bundle per test. right now it’s literally in each test function.

Ethan Park: i can refactor to build once.

Sean Gallagher: do we have `-run TestBundleVerifyTamper.*` set to run serial? go tests default parallel at package level but not within suite.

Devon Price: we turned off `t.Parallel()` previously because tests were stomping on temp dirs.

Sahana Iyer: if we make temp dirs unique per test (and base bundle read-only), we can turn `t.Parallel()` back on for mutation/verify steps.

Ethan Park: verify step reads from disk + computes hashes; parallel might be ok. but CPU-bound hashing can blow up. if we shrink fixture, it’s fine.

Jordan Lee: also we can short-circuit hashing in tests by using smaller artifacts, as discussed.

Marcus Lin: just don’t accidentally create tests that pass even if verification skips hashing. we should keep a test that ensures hash mismatch is caught.

Ethan Park: yep.

Sahana Iyer: what about caching computed hashes? manifest already contains expected hashes. verification computes actual hashes. but for test runtime, we can keep artifact sizes small.

Devon Price: also: our “base bundle” generator currently re-hashes *every file* even when we only need a few artifacts for the integration tests.

Devon Price: could create a “test bundle builder” that emits minimal layout:
- manifest.json
- manifest.sig
- charts/ (tiny)
- binaries/ (tiny)
- sbom/ pointer file

Connor O'Brien: +1. our CI should validate semantics, not the full production payload.

Sahana Iyer: ok. action items:
- Ethan: refactor suite to build base bundle once per package; use hardlink copy per test + break link on mutated file
- Devon: swap heavy fixtures (docker save) -> deterministic tiny tar/file
- Sahana: adjust Buildkite step timeouts + add timing logs (per phase)

Ethan Park: i’ll take the refactor. estimate: today + tomorrow.

Devon Price: i’ll patch fixtures now. probably behind a flag so we don’t lose the “realistic” path names.

Marcus Lin: please add a comment in code about why fixtures are small (perf) + what security properties are still validated.

Sean Gallagher: once it’s down, can we set an SLO for this job? like <12m p95 on main.

Sahana Iyer: yep. will add to infra dashboard after we stabilize.

Devon Price: quick note: deterministic tar creation should use:
`tar --sort=name --mtime='UTC 2020-01-01' --owner=0 --group=0 --numeric-owner -cf ...`
so hashes are stable.

Jordan Lee: love it. also ensure json canonicalization for manifest is stable. our tests currently write json with stdlib which doesn’t guarantee key order if map-based.

Ethan Park: base bundle generator uses our canonical writer (sorted keys). good.

Connor O'Brien: ping me if we need to change the release gate thresholds. but ideally this doesn’t block RC builds anymore.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118301 started for redwood-private-installer / branch `ethan/shared-bundle-fixture`

Sahana Iyer: thanks all. let’s regroup after we have numbers.

Ethan Park: update: refactor done locally. base bundle built once. tamper tests run in parallel with isolated dirs. runtime on my laptop: 4m -> 55s.

Devon Price: fixture patch landed too. docker save removed. the “image tar” is now 8KB deterministic file. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: reviewed the changes. looks fine. keep one test that ensures changing `images/redwood-runtime.tar` triggers `HASH_MISMATCH` and we’re good.

Sahana Iyer: awesome. once CI confirms, i’ll tighten timeout back down and add an alert if it regresses.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118301 passed
ci-bot: Step timings:
- bundle-verify-integration: 6m12s (was 33m41s)

Sahana Iyer: ✅ perf is back. i’ll file a follow-up to add a nightly “full-size bundle smoke” so we still catch weird tar/unpack issues without slowing main.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. this unblocks the release train gate. thanks.
text:
Sahana Iyer: heads up: the signature verification integration tests are now the slowest step in `private-installer` CI 😬

Sahana Iyer: `bundle-verify-integration` job went from ~6m to ~28-35m after we added tamper/replay/downgrade cases. We need to get this back under ~10m or we’re gonna start timing out in release train.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118244 for redwood-private-installer / main failed
ci-bot: Step `bundle-verify-integration` exceeded soft timeout (30m). Log: https://buildkite.example.redwood/...

Devon Price: looking at the logs now. a lot of time is `sha256sum` over big fixtures + re-generating offline bundle each test case.

Ethan Park: yeah, each test currently:
1) builds bundle (tar + generate manifest)
2) signs manifest
3) unpacks
4) modifies artifact
5) runs preflight

Ethan Park: so we’re doing the expensive steps N times.

Jordan Lee: manifest canonicalization step is also re-running every time. not huge but it adds up.

Sahana Iyer: can we make a “golden” bundle fixture once per job and then derive tampered variants cheaply?

Devon Price: +1. also the fixture includes a big image tarball (we added it to simulate offline image import). hashing that is killing us.

Connor O'Brien: release side: we don’t actually need a 3GB image tar to validate signature semantics. we need to validate that we *would* hash the referenced artifact, but the artifact can be small in tests.

Marcus Lin: from security POV, as long as the test still exercises:
- content hash validation
- signature verification
- fail-closed behavior
- downgrade protection
we can shrink the bytes.

Sean Gallagher: also worth splitting “fast unit-ish verification tests” vs “true end-to-end on a full bundle”. run the full bundle one nightly?

Ethan Park: we already have unit tests for canonicalization + envelope parsing. the integration suite is meant to be “bundle on disk + preflight CLI”.

Ethan Park: but we could do:
- one e2e uses a “medium” bundle (still realistic layout)
- tamper variants derived from that bundle via file copy + single edit

Sahana Iyer: what’s the slowest operation by flame graph / rough breakdown?

Devon Price: rough from build logs:
- `create_bundle()` ~9-12m (mostly tar + sha256s)
- `verify_bundle()` ~30-60s each
- repeated 12 times = pain

Devon Price: also `docker save` in one fixture is ~4m. why are we doing docker save here?

Ethan Park: legacy from older offline install tests. for signing, we only need to test that the manifest lists artifacts + hashes and we verify them offline.

Jordan Lee: can we replace docker save fixture with just a dummy file named `images/redwood-runtime.tar` that is small?

Marcus Lin: yes, but keep the path + semantics. the test should ensure that *changing that file* triggers hash mismatch.

Sahana Iyer: ok. proposal:
1) replace heavy image fixture with small deterministic tar (like `tar --mtime=0 --sort=name ...`)
2) generate base bundle once, cache it across test cases
3) for each test: copy base bundle dir (hardlink?) and mutate only one file

Sahana Iyer: for (2) we can do `TestMain` / shared setup if in Go.

Ethan Park: suite is in Go. we can use `sync.Once` and write base bundle into `$TMPDIR/base_bundle`.

Devon Price: copying the bundle dir might still be expensive. can we do copy-on-write? linux hardlinks via `cp -al`?

Sahana Iyer: Buildkite agents are linux, so `cp -al` should work. but careful: mutating file should break hardlink (need to copy the file you mutate, not write in-place).

Jordan Lee: for tamper cases we usually modify manifest or one artifact. we can do:
- copy tree as hardlinks
- for the file to modify, `cp` it first to break link, then edit

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Connor O'Brien: also add `BUILDKITE_CACHE`/artifact caching? we could upload the base bundle as build artifact for the step and have other steps consume it.

Sahana Iyer: within a single step we can probably avoid cross-step caching. cross-build caching is tricky (branch isolation) but possible.

Devon Price: simplest win is: stop regenerating bundle per test. right now it’s literally in each test function.

Ethan Park: i can refactor to build once.

Sean Gallagher: do we have `-run TestBundleVerifyTamper.*` set to run serial? go tests default parallel at package level but not within suite.

Devon Price: we turned off `t.Parallel()` previously because tests were stomping on temp dirs.

Sahana Iyer: if we make temp dirs unique per test (and base bundle read-only), we can turn `t.Parallel()` back on for mutation/verify steps.

Ethan Park: verify step reads from disk + computes hashes; parallel might be ok. but CPU-bound hashing can blow up. if we shrink fixture, it’s fine.

Jordan Lee: also we can short-circuit hashing in tests by using smaller artifacts, as discussed.

Marcus Lin: just don’t accidentally create tests that pass even if verification skips hashing. we should keep a test that ensures hash mismatch is caught.

Ethan Park: yep.

Sahana Iyer: what about caching computed hashes? manifest already contains expected hashes. verification computes actual hashes. but for test runtime, we can keep artifact sizes small.

Devon Price: also: our “base bundle” generator currently re-hashes *every file* even when we only need a few artifacts for the integration tests.

Devon Price: could create a “test bundle builder” that emits minimal layout:
- manifest.json
- manifest.sig
- charts/ (tiny)
- binaries/ (tiny)
- sbom/ pointer file

Connor O'Brien: +1. our CI should validate semantics, not the full production payload.

Sahana Iyer: ok. action items:
- Ethan: refactor suite to build base bundle once per package; use hardlink copy per test + break link on mutated file
- Devon: swap heavy fixtures (docker save) -> deterministic tiny tar/file
- Sahana: adjust Buildkite step timeouts + add timing logs (per phase)

Ethan Park: i’ll take the refactor. estimate: today + tomorrow.

Devon Price: i’ll patch fixtures now. probably behind a flag so we don’t lose the “realistic” path names.

Marcus Lin: please add a comment in code about why fixtures are small (perf) + what security properties are still validated.

Sean Gallagher: once it’s down, can we set an SLO for this job? like <12m p95 on main.

Sahana Iyer: yep. will add to infra dashboard after we stabilize.

Devon Price: quick note: deterministic tar creation should use:
`tar --sort=name --mtime='UTC 2020-01-01' --owner=0 --group=0 --numeric-owner -cf ...`
so hashes are stable.

Jordan Lee: love it. also ensure json canonicalization for manifest is stable. our tests currently write json with stdlib which doesn’t guarantee key order if map-based.

Ethan Park: base bundle generator uses our canonical writer (sorted keys). good.

Connor O'Brien: ping me if we need to change the release gate thresholds. but ideally this doesn’t block RC builds anymore.

ci-bot: Buildkite build #118301 started for redwood-private-installer / branch `ethan/shared-bundle-fixture`

Sahana Iyer: thanks all. let’s regroup after we have numbers.

Ethan Park: update: refactor done locally. base bundle built once. tamper tests run in parallel with isolated dirs. runtime on my laptop: 4m -> 55s.

Devon Price: fixture patch landed too. docker save removed. the “image tar” is now 8KB deterministic file. :thumbsup:

Marcus Lin: reviewed the changes. looks fine. keep one test that ensures changing `images/redwood-runtime.tar` triggers `HASH_MISMATCH` and we’re good.

Sahana Iyer: awesome. once CI confirms, i’ll tighten timeout back down and add an alert if it regresses.

ci-bot: Step timings:
- bundle-verify-integration: 6m12s (was 33m41s)

Sahana Iyer: ✅ perf is back. i’ll file a follow-up to add a nightly “full-size bundle smoke” so we still catch weird tar/unpack issues without slowing main.

Connor O'Brien: perfect. this unblocks the release train gate. thanks.

title: #eng-infra: 1755500123
preview:
Maria Gomez: FYI — noticed a ~28% bump in GPU spend for Feb compared to forecast. Most of it shows up as "untagged" in the billing export. Anyone else seeing this?
Kevin Li: can you paste the billing export link? we should run the usual BigQuery grouping.
Maria Gomez: https://console.cloud.google.com/billing/exports — I exported to bigquery this morning. top-line is GPUs in us-central1 and europe-west4 appearing under project:infra-shared.
Sachin Rao: infra-shared is our shared GPU pool for sandbox teams, right? sounds like workloads are spinning up without cost center tags.
Lina Park: yep, some onboarding scripts default to project-level creds and don't set labels. we need enforcement + backfill.
Ben Carter: quick reproduction: my local job uses the shared pool and i didn't get prompted to set label. we should default a label on the compute template.
InfraBot: Deploy 17592 succeeded in infra-shared (autoscaler v3).
Kevin Li: proposed plan: 1) run a backfill query to map untagged charges by resource id -> try to infer team from git repo/owner OR instance naming 2) add a deny policy for create without tag for new instances 3) run a weekly job to alert on >5% untagged GPU spend
Maria Gomez: agree. for (1) can we start with BigQuery and GCP resource labels? here's a query i used last time to surface GPU-hour untagged spend:
```sql
SELECT service.description, sku.description, labels, SUM(cost) as total_cost
FROM `billing_project.gcp_billing_export_v1_v2.gcp_billing_export_v1_*`
WHERE EXTRACT(MONTH FROM usage_start_time)=2 AND sku.description LIKE '%NVIDIA%'
GROUP BY service.description, sku.description, labels
ORDER BY total_cost DESC
LIMIT 200
```
Lina Park: nice. add resource.name to correlate with instance/VM id. also join with metadata table to try owner label inference.
Sachin Rao: for backfill heuristics: instance name prefix -> team mapping, project IAM last modified -> likely owner, and GCS artifact egress logs to map repos. none perfect but should get most.
Abby (Finance): Important — Finance needs a clean mapping for chargeback this quarter. if backfill is noisy we should tag ambiguous as "shared-pool" and bill to central infra budget, not product teams.
Ben Carter: +1. prefer central first then reallocate if teams accept responsibility. prevents surprise invoices.
Maria Gomez: also need to revisit reserved commitments. current committed GPU hours for us-central1 (reserved) aren't aligned to consumption — we're paying for unused reserved capacity in ap-southeast while demand is in eu-west4. Suggest: evaluate convertible/reservable commitments or move some commitment credits to region-flex if provider allows.
Kevin Li: action: run per-region 12-week utilization for GPU SKUs, output table: region, sku, reserved_hours, used_hours, utilization_pct, potential_savings_if_rightsize. i can script it.
Lina Park: note: Dedicated (reserved) pools for Dedicated customers vs shared pools for infra-shared have different billing attachments. don't mix commitments with shared ephemeral pools unless we can partition quotas.
Sachin Rao: cloud provider offers regional discount tiers at 1yr/3yr. we should model cost under current growth: if we commit to 1yr in us-central we save ~22% but could leave us exposed if workloads move. consider convertible reservations or shorter terms for volatile workloads.
Abby (Finance): run two models: conservative (no growth), expected growth (10% qtr over qtr), and aggressive (30%). show NPV and payback period. finance can sign off on a 1-yr commit if payback <9 months.
Maria Gomez: For chargeback mechanics: propose tagging schema: cost_center, product, env, owner, pool. enforce cost_center and owner on any GPU allocation. Also add an automated Slack notify when pool usage spikes > threshold and list top 5 untagged instances.
Kevin Li: we already have a Cloud Function that runs on new instance create — i'll add label enforcement and auto-reject if missing (with a bypass for infra-admin role and an exception request flow).
Ben Carter: for existing VMs: i'll run a backfill job that writes inferred labels to a staging table and creates PRs to owners for confirmation. PRs will be auto-approved after 14 days and then labels applied.
InfraBot: Alert: untagged-gpu-hours > 50 in last 24h (infra-shared).
Maria Gomez: ok timeline: Kevin + Ben do backfill + enforcement this week. Sachin to produce 12-week utilization report by Friday. Abby will run commit financial models by next Wed. Lina will update ops runbook and add exception flow.
Lina Park: raising one constraint — network egress between regions inflates cross-region cost, so moving workloads between regions to match commitments may introduce net-negative savings. account for inter-region egress in modeling.
Sachin Rao: good catch. i'll include estimated egress costs per TB between candidate regions.
Abby (Finance): please add a column for tax & marketplace fees; they vary by region.
Maria Gomez: owners: @Kevin Li (labels + enforcement), @Ben Carter (backfill), @Sachin Rao (utilization + egress modeling), @Abby (financial models), @Lina Park (runbook). ping me if blocked. :thumbsup:
Kevin Li: i'll create the PRs and a small helm chart change for default labels, linking to enforcement policy.
Ben Carter: starting backfill job now, will post results in this thread when the first pass finishes.
Maria Gomez: thanks all, want this done before next billing close.
Ben Carter: first-pass results: 62% of untagged GPU-hours were attributable to 3 project IDs (sandbox-*, experiments-*). leaving the rest as shared-pool. I'll upload csv to gs://infra-ops/chargeback/backfill-1755500123.csv and post a summary in #eng-infra-next-week.
Maria Gomez: perfect, let's review on THU 11:00am.
