title: #eng-infra: 1749189022
preview:
Vanessa Ortiz: Need to lock capacity reservations for benchmark sweeps in eu-west + APAC. @Jonas Weber @Rina Kobayashi can you confirm model list + rough run window next week?

Jonas Weber: Yep. Publishing regions: us-east, eu-west, apac-sg. Models: Llama 3.1 8B/70B, Qwen2.5 7B/72B, Mixtral 8x7B. We want to start sweeps Mon-Wed and rerun if stability gate trips.

Rina Kobayashi: Runner can do region sweep matrix but I need to know what hardware we’re standardizing on per model. If we mix instance types across regions, p95 variance gets messy.

Vanessa Ortiz: That’s the main issue: eu-west has decent H100 coverage; apac-sg is constrained on H100 and the A100 pool is fragmented.

Harish Reddy: Adding current fleet reality:
- eu-west: h100x8 nodes available (80GB), plus a100x8 (80GB) as fallback
- apac-sg: limited h100x8, more a100x8 but several are already allocated to customer Dedicated
- apac-tokyo: basically no spare H100 next week (maintenance + existing commits)

Claire Dubois: For 70B/72B we can run TP=2 on H100 80GB fine. On A100 80GB TP=2 is OK but we may need to lower max batch / be careful on long context.

Mateo Alvarez: Runtime POV: if we do 70B/72B on A100 in apac-sg but H100 in eu-west, the “latency/$” story will be questioned. Ideally same GPU class across publish regions.

Vanessa Ortiz: Agree. But if we insist on H100 in apac-sg we might only get a narrow window overnight local.

Jonas Weber: Overnight is fine. We can schedule. We mainly need deterministic throughput + minimal queueing noise.

Sean Gallagher: +1 please don’t run during APAC business hours if we’re sharing pool with hosted traffic. Also we need to pre-warm caches / avoid thundering herd on the cluster.

Harish Reddy: Proposed reservation plan (draft):
- eu-west: reserve 2x h100x8 nodes for 48h (Mon 00:00 UTC -> Wed 00:00 UTC)
- apac-sg: reserve 1x h100x8 node for 12h blocks (Mon/Tue 18:00-06:00 SGT)
If APAC H100 slips, fallback is a100x8 but we annotate + potentially keep APAC as “directional” until we can rerun on H100.

Rina Kobayashi: If apac-sg switches to A100, I’d rather hold publish and keep the run internal. Otherwise every external person will ask “why is APAC worse.”

Vanessa Ortiz: Understood. Let’s treat A100 fallback as emergency to unblock harness testing, not publish.

Neha Kapoor: Decision needed: are we OK delaying APAC publish if H100 doesn’t free up? I’d prefer to publish fewer regions than publish shaky data.

Jonas Weber: Yes. We can do internal APAC A100 run as a sanity check but only publish APAC once we get clean H100 sweep.

Vanessa Ortiz: Cool. Then I’ll request H100 only for apac-sg.

Harish Reddy: Instance type constraint callout: apac-sg h100x8 are split across two racks; cross-rack latency isn’t an issue for inference but if you’re doing multi-node it matters. Please keep TP within one node (no multi-node tensor parallel).

Claire Dubois: We’re single-node TP only. No multi-node.

Mateo Alvarez: For Mixtral 8x7B: it’s more sensitive to batching. Can we reserve enough headroom so we don’t hit queueing? I’d like to keep utilization <70% during measurement.

Jonas Weber: Concurrency target is 32–64 depending on workload slice. We can run in “steady state” and sample for ~20 mins per configuration. We can cap at 60% GPU util.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max QPS limiter on the benchmark runners so a misconfig doesn’t hammer the shared control plane.

Rina Kobayashi: I can set `--qps-cap` and `--max-inflight` per runner. Also will randomize start offsets to avoid synchronized bursts.

Vanessa Ortiz: Ok, what’s the exact node count needed? If you reserve 1x h100x8 in apac-sg, can you run all models sequentially on that one node?

Jonas Weber: Yes, sequential is fine. We just need time. 8B/7B can even run on a single GPU but we’ll keep the same node class for consistency.

Harish Reddy: One more constraint: apac-sg has only 1 spare h100x8 at the moment and it’s currently earmarked for a potential Dedicated expansion. I can block it for the benchmark window but we need sign-off from @Neha Kapoor.

Neha Kapoor: Approved for the Mon/Tue overnight blocks as long as we release by Wed 06:00 SGT. If Sales screams, we may need to adjust.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: Thanks. I’ll put in the reservation and label it “BENCHMARK_SWEEP_APAC_SG_H100” so it doesn’t get reclaimed.

Noah Patel: Quick check: do we have eu-west H100 of the same stepping/driver as apac-sg? Kernel selection differences have bitten us before.

Harish Reddy: eu-west H100 pool is on driver 550.54; apac-sg is 550.54 as well. CUDA minor versions match. We’re good.

Rina Kobayashi: Great. I’ll pin runner image digest anyway (dockerized runner) so deps are deterministic.

Vanessa Ortiz: Action items:
- Harish: file the reservations in fleet tool for eu-west 2x h100x8 (Mon-Wed UTC) + apac-sg 1x h100x8 (Mon/Tue 18:00-06:00 SGT)
- Jonas/Rina: confirm sweep duration estimate + send me the manifest matrix so I can sanity check runtime
- Sean: share the launch-week oncall window so we don’t collide with any planned maintenance

Sean Gallagher: No maintenance planned. But please post in #eng-oncall when runners start, so we know if alerts are benchmark-related.

Jonas Weber: Will do. Also: if apac-sg H100 becomes unavailable, we pause APAC publish and only publish us-east + eu-west in v1.

Vanessa Ortiz: Works. I’ll update the capacity doc + drop a note in the GTM thread so PMM isn’t surprised.

Harish Reddy: Reservations submitted. Pending approval in fleet system for eu-west H100 because one node is currently “warm spare” for an enterprise customer. I’ll resolve today.

Vanessa Ortiz: thx. Keep me posted if eu-west drops to 1 node; we can still run but reruns will take longer.

Rina Kobayashi: FYI runner settings I’m planning:
`--region eu-west --node-type h100x8 --qps-cap 40 --max-inflight 256 --warmup-minutes 15 --sample-minutes 20`
Same for apac-sg. Will tune if we see queueing.

Claire Dubois: Looks good. Please ensure we capture “capacity reserved” in run manifest so we can state results weren’t affected by noisy neighbors.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ll include that in the methodology notes.

Vanessa Ortiz: Closing loop: once fleet approvals clear I’ll paste reservation IDs here.

Harish Reddy: Instance type constraint callout: apac-sg h100x8 are split across two racks; cross-rack latency isn’t an issue for inference but if you’re doing multi-node it matters. Please keep TP within one node (no multi-node tensor parallel).

Claire Dubois: We’re single-node TP only. No multi-node.

Mateo Alvarez: For Mixtral 8x7B: it’s more sensitive to batching. Can we reserve enough headroom so we don’t hit queueing? I’d like to keep utilization <70% during measurement.

Jonas Weber: Concurrency target is 32–64 depending on workload slice. We can run in “steady state” and sample for ~20 mins per configuration. We can cap at 60% GPU util.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max QPS limiter on the benchmark runners so a misconfig doesn’t hammer the shared control plane.

Rina Kobayashi: I can set `--qps-cap` and `--max-inflight` per runner. Also will randomize start offsets to avoid synchronized bursts.

Vanessa Ortiz: Ok, what’s the exact node count needed? If you reserve 1x h100x8 in apac-sg, can you run all models sequentially on that one node?

Jonas Weber: Yes, sequential is fine. We just need time. 8B/7B can even run on a single GPU but we’ll keep the same node class for consistency.

Harish Reddy: One more constraint: apac-sg has only 1 spare h100x8 at the moment and it’s currently earmarked for a potential Dedicated expansion. I can block it for the benchmark window but we need sign-off from @Neha Kapoor.

Neha Kapoor: Approved for the Mon/Tue overnight blocks as long as we release by Wed 06:00 SGT. If Sales screams, we may need to adjust.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: Thanks. I’ll put in the reservation and label it “BENCHMARK_SWEEP_APAC_SG_H100” so it doesn’t get reclaimed.

Noah Patel: Quick check: do we have eu-west H100 of the same stepping/driver as apac-sg? Kernel selection differences have bitten us before.

Harish Reddy: eu-west H100 pool is on driver 550.54; apac-sg is 550.54 as well. CUDA minor versions match. We’re good.

Rina Kobayashi: Great. I’ll pin runner image digest anyway (dockerized runner) so deps are deterministic.

Vanessa Ortiz: Action items:
- Harish: file the reservations in fleet tool for eu-west 2x h100x8 (Mon-Wed UTC) + apac-sg 1x h100x8 (Mon/Tue 18:00-06:00 SGT)
- Jonas/Rina: confirm sweep duration estimate + send me the manifest matrix so I can sanity check runtime
- Sean: share the launch-week oncall window so we don’t collide with any planned maintenance

Sean Gallagher: No maintenance planned. But please post in #eng-oncall when runners start, so we know if alerts are benchmark-related.

Jonas Weber: Will do. Also: if apac-sg H100 becomes unavailable, we pause APAC publish and only publish us-east + eu-west in v1.

Vanessa Ortiz: Works. I’ll update the capacity doc + drop a note in the GTM thread so PMM isn’t surprised.

Harish Reddy: Reservations submitted. Pending approval in fleet system for eu-west H100 because one node is currently “warm spare” for an enterprise customer. I’ll resolve today.

Vanessa Ortiz: thx. Keep me posted if eu-west drops to 1 node; we can still run but reruns will take longer.

Rina Kobayashi: FYI runner settings I’m planning:
`--region eu-west --node-type h100x8 --qps-cap 40 --max-inflight 256 --warmup-minutes 15 --sample-minutes 20`
Same for apac-sg. Will tune if we see queueing.

Claire Dubois: Looks good. Please ensure we capture “capacity reserved” in run manifest so we can state results weren’t affected by noisy neighbors.

Jonas Weber: +1. We’ll include that in the methodology notes.

Vanessa Ortiz: Closing loop: once fleet approvals clear I’ll paste reservation IDs here.

title: #eng-infra: 1748572103
preview:
Vanessa Ortiz: kicking off a quick thread to de-risk bounds implementation before we merge the autoscaler changes. Need alignment on (1) edge cases + semantics (2) defaulting strategy for existing Dedicated deployments (3) who owns migration/backfill. I’m looking at ENG-22140 + ENG-22153.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. I’m in the autoscaler loop code now. Biggest question: do we expect control plane to always send explicit `min_replicas` / `max_replicas`, or can they be null? i can clamp either way but need deterministic behavior.

Benji Okafor: schema PR (ENG-22141) is leaning nullable fields: `bounds_enabled` + `min_replicas?` + `max_replicas?` + cooldowns. Validation: if bounds_enabled=true then require both min/max and enforce min<=max. If bounds_enabled=false, we treat min/max as ignored even if present.

Jordan Lee: from policy engine POV, we can compute an “effective policy” snapshot that the autoscaler reads. Proposal:
- if feature flag off OR bounds_enabled=false => `effective_min`/`effective_max` = empty, autoscaler uses legacy behavior
- if bounds_enabled=true => clamp desired replicas into [min,max]
We should also emit a reason string for audit/logging: `clamp:min` / `clamp:max` / `no-bounds`.

Vanessa Ortiz: ok but we still need to decide what happens to *existing* deployments when we first roll out schema. we can’t suddenly require min/max everywhere.

Michael Nguyen: on migration/backfill: I can add columns/fields with null defaults and backfill policy rows in a “legacy mode” so reads don’t break. But we need to decide what value to set for existing deployments in DB.

Ethan Park: strong preference: do NOT backfill min/max for existing customers. Too risky (we’ll guess wrong). Instead:
- add new policy object w/ bounds_enabled=false by default
- only set bounds_enabled=true when explicitly enabled via console/api + feature flag
That preserves behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: works for me as long as autoscaler treats “no policy / legacy policy” identically. I’ll code defensively: if policy missing or bounds_enabled=false => skip clamp.

Rafael Mendes: SRE lens: please avoid any migration that changes fleet behavior without explicit enablement. If we do backfill, it should be a no-op. Also we need a clear story on “max below current replicas” if a customer tightens bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: yeah, edge cases list i want to cover:
1) min > max (should be rejected)
2) max < current_replicas on update
3) min > current_replicas on update
4) min=0 (do we allow scale-to-zero?)
5) predictor missing/stale; predictive wants 0 or null
6) region shortage: can’t meet min

Benji Okafor: (1) will be API validation error 400 w/ field-level message.
Re (4) min=0: I’d allow only if deployment has `allow_scale_to_zero=true` (we don’t expose that broadly for Dedicated today). Otherwise enforce min>=1.

Jordan Lee: +1. For Dedicated, scale-to-zero is basically never appropriate (baseline SLO + warm cache). We can have `min_replicas >= 1` as validation for Dedicated deployments.

Keiko Yamamoto: (2) if max < current, what’s desired? immediate downscale to max? or just prevent scale-ups and let reactive scale-down eventually bring it down?

Rafael Mendes: please do not immediately drop capacity on config change unless user explicitly asked and we can do it safely. But also: the customer *is* setting a hard ceiling, so we can’t keep running above max indefinitely.

Ethan Park: semantics I’d propose:
- ceiling is enforced on scale-up decisions immediately (never increase above max)
- if currently above max, autoscaler should schedule a controlled scale-down toward max, subject to cooldown + “safe scale-down” constraints (no step-down bigger than X per interval)
We should document that it may take up to N minutes to converge.

Vanessa Ortiz: i like that. Keiko can you implement “converge down to max” with dampening? We already have cooldown plumbing in the loop.

Keiko Yamamoto: yep. I’ll add:
- if current > max: treat desired = max (then pass through existing scale-down guards + cooldown)
- step size cap on downscale (e.g. 20% or at least 1 replica)
- emit `bound_exceeded_current` metric so dashboard shows “running above max” state.

Samantha Roy: for telemetry: can we standardize metrics now? I can wire:
- `autoscaler_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas`
- `autoscaler_bound_hit_total{bound=min|max}`
- `autoscaler_current_above_max` gauge
Need labels: customer_id, deployment_id, region (with cardinality guard).

Vanessa Ortiz: yes, this thread is exactly to pin that down. Also (6) region shortage: if min cannot be met, autoscaler will keep requesting min, but scheduler may not place. We need degraded surfacing.

Rafael Mendes: + alert. “min unmet for >10m” is customer-impacting. But make sure we don’t page on transient placement delays.

Jordan Lee: policy engine can include a `min_unmet_behavior` later, but for v1 simplest:
- autoscaler continues to request min
- control plane marks deployment status `DEGRADED_MIN_UNMET` when provisioning fails with capacity shortage signal
- emit event + metric

Michael Nguyen: ownership question: who’s writing the backfill job? I can handle DB migration + backfill to set `bounds_enabled=false` for all existing Dedicated deployments. But I need the canonical definition of “existing deployment has no record” vs “record w/ bounds_enabled=false” for the autoscaler.

Keiko Yamamoto: from my side either works. If no record: treat as legacy. If record exists but bounds_enabled=false: treat as legacy. Just don’t 500.

Ethan Park: let’s do explicit record on backfill so console reads something consistent. But content should be equivalent to legacy behavior. i.e. bounds_enabled=false, predictive_enabled=false, cooldown fields default to current platform defaults.

Benji Okafor: careful: if we backfill cooldowns, we might accidentally change tuning. Suggest leaving cooldown fields null too and have the autoscaler apply its legacy defaults unless bounds_enabled/predictive_enabled flips on.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. legacy defaults should stay in autoscaler config for now. Only apply policy cooldowns when enabled.

Michael Nguyen: ok, plan:
- migration adds new policy table/fields
- backfill creates policy rows for existing Dedicated deployments with `bounds_enabled=false`, `predictive_enabled=false`, other fields null
- also write an audit log event `scaling_policy_initialized`? or skip to avoid noise?

Rafael Mendes: skip audit events for backfill IMO (or mark actor as `system` and suppress customer-visible export). Compliance folks might want it recorded but not spammy.

Jordan Lee: we can do: actor=`system/migration`, reason=`schema_backfill`, and in export pipeline we can filter those from default customer views. (Kevin Osei asked for that pattern in another thread.)

Vanessa Ortiz: good. last big one: predictor returning null/stale. We’ve seen “predictor says 0” in earlier prototypes. We need safe behavior.

Keiko Yamamoto: I’ll implement: if predictive_enabled=true and predictor is missing/stale/null => fall back to reactive desired (no predictive bias). Also NEVER treat null as 0. (I’ll add unit test; this bit me before :/)

Ethan Park: can we make that deterministic and logged? i.e. reason `prediction_unavailable_fallback=reactive`.

Samantha Roy: yep, I can add structured log field `prediction_state={ok|stale|null|error}`.

Jamal Pierce: on defaulting: do we want a “recommended range” shown in Console even if bounds disabled? Product is asking. That’s separate from enforcement, but we should not conflate recommended with hard bounds.

Vanessa Ortiz: agreed: recommended range is UI only. hard bounds are enforcement. We’ll keep them separate.

Rafael Mendes: action items:
- Vanessa/Keiko: confirm semantics doc for edge cases (max < current, min unmet)
- Michael: own migration/backfill implementation (ENG-22153)
- Benji/Jordan: lock validation rules (min>=1 for Dedicated, min<=max, bounds_enabled gating)
- Samantha: metric names + labels in line w/ observability standards

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: i’ll summarize in ENG-22140 and link this thread. Keiko, once you have a PR up, tag me + Rafael for review (esp. the “currently above max converge down” behavior).

Keiko Yamamoto: will do. aiming EOD tomorrow for the clamp + metrics + edge-case tests.

Michael Nguyen: starting migration branch now. I’ll coordinate with Benji on schema shape so I don’t drift.

Benji Okafor: i’ll drop the latest protobuf/json schema snippet here once it’s in review.

Vanessa Ortiz: thanks all.

title: #eng-infra: 1747211044
preview:
Bruno Silva: Heads up: Terraform module changes for KMS are ready for review. This is the follow-on to the least-privilege work + rotation role split. Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-terraform/pull/611 ("add least-privilege KMS IAM modules").

Bruno Silva: TL;DR
- New submodules: `kms_runtime_role` (Decrypt/DataKey) and `kms_rotation_role` (DescribeKey + CreateGrant/PutKeyPolicy bits depending on cloud).
- Default behavior stays close to existing, but we *will* add new outputs and a couple of variables.
- Need to decide version bump + compatibility notes for existing Private customers.

Jordan Lee: looking now. Do we currently have customers pinning the module version in their Private deploy repos? If yes, we need to be careful about a breaking major.

Bruno Silva: yep, most Private customers pin `redwood/private` terraform modules. Some are pinned by installer-generated `versions.tf` too. That’s why I’m trying to avoid a surprise break.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: the big concern is rollout sequence. If we ship module changes that tighten IAM and someone applies before the runtime wrapper cutover is fully deployed, we get decrypt failures.

Bruno Silva: +1. In PR I kept the existing policy attachments as defaults, and added a new `enable_separate_rotation_principal` flag (default false) so we don’t force split principals immediately.

Jordan Lee: I see it. Variable name: `separate_rotation_principal_enabled` in one file and `enable_separate_rotation_principal` in README. Can we standardize? :)

Bruno Silva: good catch, will fix.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we should still message this as “recommended split roles” and call out that rotation actions should not run under the runtime decrypt principal. But I’m ok with defaulting to current behavior for compatibility.

Allison Grant: also please make sure module exports the role ARN consistently. Observability hooks expect the label `kms_principal` to match what we document (role ARN string).

Bruno Silva: module outputs right now:
- existing: `kms_role_arn`
- new: `kms_runtime_role_arn`, `kms_rotation_role_arn`
I left `kms_role_arn` as an alias to runtime for now.

Jordan Lee: that alias helps. Please document that `kms_role_arn` is deprecated but still present.

Vivek Kulkarni: for Private: installer will generate terraform config. We can update installer templates to use the new outputs, but we need a path for existing customers who don’t upgrade installer immediately.

Bruno Silva: exactly. Proposed compatibility plan:
1) Terraform module release: minor bump (e.g. 3.6.0 -> 3.7.0) w/ new roles behind flag, keep old outputs.
2) Installer update references new outputs but continues to accept old variables if present.
3) After 1-2 quarters, we can do a major bump that removes deprecated outputs.

Connor O'Brien: release process question: do we publish these modules with semver tags? if so, we should treat output removals as major.

Bruno Silva: yes, internal semver tags. That’s why I’m leaning minor now.

Sean Gallagher: can we also add a “safety check” note: apply in staging first; verify decrypt calls before tightening? People will copy/paste.

Bruno Silva: yep. Adding README section: “Upgrade notes (Private existing customers)” with a checklist:
- Ensure KMS wrapper + circuit breaker changes deployed (>= runtime build X)
- Apply terraform
- Confirm dashboards: KMS AccessDenied=0, latency stable
- Then enable `enable_separate_rotation_principal=true` in a follow-up change

Jordan Lee: please be explicit about permissions.
Runtime principal should need:
- `kms:Decrypt`
- `kms:GenerateDataKey` (or `GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext` depending)
Rotation principal should need:
- `kms:DescribeKey`
- and whatever is used to update alias/key versioning from our control plane (we generally don’t want `kms:PutKeyPolicy` unless customer model requires it)

Marcus Lin: +1. Also mention: customers may have SCPs / org policies; we should avoid adding optional permissions by default.

Bruno Silva: PR currently uses a conservative base policy and exposes `extra_kms_actions_runtime` / `extra_kms_actions_rotation` lists if needed.

Allison Grant: any renames to metric tags needed? If the role changes, we should ensure dashboards don’t break. But that’s probably on the app side.

Sean Gallagher: oncall pain point: customers apply Terraform and then file a ticket with an AccessDenied. If we can add a note: “common failure mode: key policy doesn’t allow new role ARN” that’d save cycles.

Bruno Silva: adding to README troubleshooting:
- “AccessDenied after applying: update key policy to trust the runtime role ARN; if enabling split roles, ensure both ARNs are trusted during transition.”

Vivek Kulkarni: can we align this with installer preflight validation? The installer PR adds checks (I think PR-392). If terraform changes cause new role ARN, preflight should warn if key policy doesn’t include it.

Bruno Silva: yep, I’ll sync w/ that. https://github.com/redwood-private-installer/pull/392 is the one.

Jordan Lee: one more: module currently changes the default session name / role path? Some customers rely on predictable IAM paths.

Bruno Silva: no role path change. Names will be stable unless `name_prefix` differs. I did add suffixes `-runtime` and `-rotation` when split enabled.

Connor O'Brien: what’s the plan for existing customers who already have a single role and don’t want terraform to replace it? Will enabling split force replacement?

Bruno Silva: enabling split creates *additional* role resources, doesn’t destroy existing. If they keep split disabled, resources are unchanged.

Sean Gallagher: good. Also please avoid “force new” on the role by changing assume role policy unexpectedly.

Bruno Silva: no assume role policy changes when split disabled.

Marcus Lin: version bump proposal sounds right: minor bump now with deprecation warnings.

Jordan Lee: can you add a changelog entry that explicitly says “no breaking changes if you don’t enable split”? People skim.

Bruno Silva: will do.

Allison Grant: if we can get a single canonical snippet for Private upgrade docs, I can link it from the KMS dashboards page too.

Bruno Silva: I’ll paste draft snippet here once README updates are in.

Sean Gallagher: request: include a note about staged rollout: enable split roles only after verifying dual-read / rotation window logic if they’re doing key rotation same week.

Bruno Silva: added note: “Don’t combine IAM tightening + key rotation cutover in the same maintenance window unless you have rollback.”

Vivek Kulkarni: :thumbsup: also note some customers do quarterly rotations w/ audit evidence; they’ll ask if this changes their rotation procedure.

Marcus Lin: we can answer: “no change required; split roles is recommended hardening; rotation procedure is the same, just uses different principal.”

Bruno Silva: ok, action items from this thread:
- Fix variable naming consistency
- Add README: upgrade notes, troubleshooting, staged rollout checklist
- Add changelog entry for minor bump
- Confirm no unexpected role replacement
I’ll push updates and re-request review on PR-611 today.

Jordan Lee: thanks. I’ll do detailed policy review once updated.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll skim for rollout hazards / defaults.

Connor O'Brien: once merged, ping me and I’ll coordinate tagging + internal announcement so Private folks don’t get surprised.

Bruno Silva: will do.

Bruno Silva: +1. In PR I kept the existing policy attachments as defaults, and added a new `enable_separate_rotation_principal` flag (default false) so we don’t force split principals immediately.

Jordan Lee: I see it. Variable name: `separate_rotation_principal_enabled` in one file and `enable_separate_rotation_principal` in README. Can we standardize? :)

Bruno Silva: good catch, will fix.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we should still message this as “recommended split roles” and call out that rotation actions should not run under the runtime decrypt principal. But I’m ok with defaulting to current behavior for compatibility.

Allison Grant: also please make sure module exports the role ARN consistently. Observability hooks expect the label `kms_principal` to match what we document (role ARN string).

Bruno Silva: module outputs right now:
- existing: `kms_role_arn`
- new: `kms_runtime_role_arn`, `kms_rotation_role_arn`
I left `kms_role_arn` as an alias to runtime for now.

Jordan Lee: that alias helps. Please document that `kms_role_arn` is deprecated but still present.

Vivek Kulkarni: for Private: installer will generate terraform config. We can update installer templates to use the new outputs, but we need a path for existing customers who don’t upgrade installer immediately.

Bruno Silva: exactly. Proposed compatibility plan:
1) Terraform module release: minor bump (e.g. 3.6.0 -> 3.7.0) w/ new roles behind flag, keep old outputs.
2) Installer update references new outputs but continues to accept old variables if present.
3) After 1-2 quarters, we can do a major bump that removes deprecated outputs.

Connor O'Brien: release process question: do we publish these modules with semver tags? if so, we should treat output removals as major.

Bruno Silva: yes, internal semver tags. That’s why I’m leaning minor now.

Sean Gallagher: can we also add a “safety check” note: apply in staging first; verify decrypt calls before tightening? People will copy/paste.

Bruno Silva: yep. Adding README section: “Upgrade notes (Private existing customers)” with a checklist:
- Ensure KMS wrapper + circuit breaker changes deployed (>= runtime build X)
- Apply terraform
- Confirm dashboards: KMS AccessDenied=0, latency stable
- Then enable `enable_separate_rotation_principal=true` in a follow-up change

Jordan Lee: please be explicit about permissions.
Runtime principal should need:
- `kms:Decrypt`
- `kms:GenerateDataKey` (or `GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext` depending)
Rotation principal should need:
- `kms:DescribeKey`
- and whatever is used to update alias/key versioning from our control plane (we generally don’t want `kms:PutKeyPolicy` unless customer model requires it)

Marcus Lin: +1. Also mention: customers may have SCPs / org policies; we should avoid adding optional permissions by default.

Bruno Silva: PR currently uses a conservative base policy and exposes `extra_kms_actions_runtime` / `extra_kms_actions_rotation` lists if needed.

Allison Grant: any renames to metric tags needed? If the role changes, we should ensure dashboards don’t break. But that’s probably on the app side.

Sean Gallagher: oncall pain point: customers apply Terraform and then file a ticket with an AccessDenied. If we can add a note: “common failure mode: key policy doesn’t allow new role ARN” that’d save cycles.

Bruno Silva: adding to README troubleshooting:
- “AccessDenied after applying: update key policy to trust the runtime role ARN; if enabling split roles, ensure both ARNs are trusted during transition.”

Vivek Kulkarni: can we align this with installer preflight validation? The installer PR adds checks (I think PR-392). If terraform changes cause new role ARN, preflight should warn if key policy doesn’t include it.

Bruno Silva: yep, I’ll sync w/ that. https://github.com/redwood-private-installer/pull/392 is the one.

Jordan Lee: one more: module currently changes the default session name / role path? Some customers rely on predictable IAM paths.

Bruno Silva: no role path change. Names will be stable unless `name_prefix` differs. I did add suffixes `-runtime` and `-rotation` when split enabled.

Connor O'Brien: what’s the plan for existing customers who already have a single role and don’t want terraform to replace it? Will enabling split force replacement?

Bruno Silva: enabling split creates *additional* role resources, doesn’t destroy existing. If they keep split disabled, resources are unchanged.

Sean Gallagher: good. Also please avoid “force new” on the role by changing assume role policy unexpectedly.

Bruno Silva: no assume role policy changes when split disabled.

Marcus Lin: version bump proposal sounds right: minor bump now with deprecation warnings.

Jordan Lee: can you add a changelog entry that explicitly says “no breaking changes if you don’t enable split”? People skim.

Bruno Silva: will do.

Allison Grant: if we can get a single canonical snippet for Private upgrade docs, I can link it from the KMS dashboards page too.

Bruno Silva: I’ll paste draft snippet here once README updates are in.

Sean Gallagher: request: include a note about staged rollout: enable split roles only after verifying dual-read / rotation window logic if they’re doing key rotation same week.

Bruno Silva: added note: “Don’t combine IAM tightening + key rotation cutover in the same maintenance window unless you have rollback.”

Vivek Kulkarni: :thumbsup: also note some customers do quarterly rotations w/ audit evidence; they’ll ask if this changes their rotation procedure.

Marcus Lin: we can answer: “no change required; split roles is recommended hardening; rotation procedure is the same, just uses different principal.”

Bruno Silva: ok, action items from this thread:
- Fix variable naming consistency
- Add README: upgrade notes, troubleshooting, staged rollout checklist
- Add changelog entry for minor bump
- Confirm no unexpected role replacement
I’ll push updates and re-request review on PR-611 today.

Jordan Lee: thanks. I’ll do detailed policy review once updated.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll skim for rollout hazards / defaults.

Connor O'Brien: once merged, ping me and I’ll coordinate tagging + internal announcement so Private folks don’t get surprised.

Bruno Silva: will do.

title: #eng-infra: 1747075433
preview:
Connor O'Brien: quick q for min-pack: do we *need* OTLP log export in v1, or is “supported sinks = Splunk HEC + Elastic/OpenSearch + (maybe) Loki” enough? trying to lock scope for the release branch.

Logan Wright: depends what we mean by “OTLP”. If we ship an otel-collector in the cluster and say “send to your OTLP endpoint”, that’s a whole support surface (auth, retries, tls, backpressure, perf). For v1 I’d rather standardize on 1 collector + 2-3 explicit outputs.

Colin O'Donnell: +1. from customer calls: 80% ask “Splunk HEC?” / “Elastic?” and then “can you not break our cluster”. OTLP comes up mostly as “we already have an otel pipeline” but they usually can adapt if we give them JSON logs and a stable namespace.

Allison Grant: reminder: standards doc currently says “provide export options suitable for private envs (SIEM/Splunk/ELK/Loki/OTLP endpoints)” but it doesn’t say *all* are required for v1. We can define OTLP as “documented integration” vs “supported”.

Rafael Mendes: from oncall POV: OTLP = lots of “why is nothing showing up” tickets. Splunk HEC and Elastic are already in the support playbooks, easier to reproduce.

Caleb Johnson: question: are we talking logs only, or traces too? OTLP for traces is even more contentious. v1 scope says “where feasible traces”, but we’ve been treating traces as best-effort.

Connor O'Brien: mainly logs. but I’m getting push from a couple folks that “OTLP is the standard”, we should do it right.

Diego Santos: packaging angle: air-gapped installs. If we ship otel-collector we have to pin image versions + config, and customers will inevitably tweak. Then upgrades get fun.

Neha Kapoor: also security review: OTLP endpoints often require mTLS and customer-managed cert chains. That’s totally doable but it becomes a long tail. Splunk HEC also has TLS but it’s a narrower config surface.

Markus Klein: fwiw from SE side: enterprise platform teams like OTLP because it plugs into existing observability pipelines. But if we can say “we ship vector/fluent-bit collector; here’s a tested recipe to forward to your OTLP gateway”, that may satisfy without us owning every permutation.

Logan Wright: what if we split:
- v1 “Supported sinks”: Splunk HEC, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, Loki (if we keep it)
- “Experimental/Documented”: OTLP HTTP (logs only) behind a values flag, no SLA
- traces: explicitly out-of-scope for minimum pack (or “off by default”)

Allison Grant: I like that, but wording matters. “Supported” should mean Support can triage w/ known-good configs. “Documented” can be “we don’t guarantee, but here’s an example.”

Colin O'Donnell: +1 and for OTLP we should limit to *one* mode. OTLP/HTTP is simpler than OTLP/gRPC in locked-down networks. Also keep auth methods minimal (bearer token + tls).

Caleb Johnson: OTLP/HTTP for logs is reasonable. But if we do it, please cap batch sizes + retry to avoid the “collector OOMs because endpoint is down” scenario. We saw that in hosted.

Rafael Mendes: :point_up: and default to “drop on backpressure” vs “block app” (or at least make it very explicit). I don’t want inference latency coupled to a customer SIEM outage.

Diego Santos: if we add OTLP, we need installer preflight checks? like DNS reachability / NetworkPolicy egress? otherwise people will enable it and it silently fails.

Connor O'Brien: for min pack I’d rather not add more preflights. already have storage + scrape targets + grafana ds auth checks.

Neha Kapoor: also: log content. if OTLP is enabled and customer ships logs out, they need the same redaction guarantees. So any OTLP config must not encourage exporting request payloads etc. (same as splunk/elk)

Logan Wright: ok, proposal:
1) v1 GA: Splunk HEC + Elastic/OpenSearch are “Supported export targets”
2) Loki is “Supported” only if we ship it as part of bundle (still TBD)
3) OTLP/HTTP logs: “Documented integration (beta)” with strict schema + limited knobs; no preflight; off by default
4) Traces: not in minimum pack v1 (mention future)

Allison Grant: works. please make sure docs clearly list support boundaries and have a troubleshooting section: “how to see collector queue/backpressure metrics”, “how to verify export attempts”.

Colin O'Donnell: can we add one line to docs: “If you already run your own otel collector, prefer scraping kubernetes logs and forwarding yourself; our supported path is Splunk/Elastic.” sets expectation.

Caleb Johnson: if we do the beta OTLP, I can add a sample config snippet and make sure we emit collector metrics (exporter errors, dropped logs) into Prometheus. That at least gives us signals.

Rafael Mendes: + runbook link in alert annotations. even for beta, we should avoid silent failure.

Connor O'Brien: cool. I’ll update release brief + scope doc: OTLP not required for v1, beta doc-only integration acceptable. owners?

Logan Wright: I’ll own the wording + values schema change.

Caleb Johnson: I’ll own the sample OTLP/HTTP config + metrics exposure (if we have time).

Colin O'Donnell: I’ll sanity check against customer integration matrix + make sure Splunk/Elastic stays the headline.

Neha Kapoor: tag me on the PRs touching export + retention so security can review.

Allison Grant: please also add a note about label/cardinality if you add exporter metrics; keep it bounded.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: locking this for v1: Splunk/ELK supported; OTLP optional beta. I’ll drop a comment on the Linear ticket and proceed with branch plan.

title: #eng-infra: 1746384109
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to land a default Prometheus TSDB sizing + retention rec for Private min pack. What are we comfortable shipping as "sane defaults" for typical footprints? Mostly concerned about PVC size + churn when folks have 15s scrapes.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Support keeps getting "Prometheus disk full" escalations, and we don’t have consistent guidance. Can we target 7d retention as default and document 15d/30d options?

Allison Grant: From standards side: minimum pack should be conservative on cardinality, but customers will still have node exporter + kube metrics + our runtime metrics. Default should prioritize stability over long retention. 7d time-based retention is fine, but we should also recommend `retention.size` as a guardrail.

Logan Wright: Agree. Also reminder: some Private installs use smaller clusters / single AZ w/ standard storage class. We should avoid recommending huge PVCs by default if we can.

Diego Santos: What are we calling "typical"? I’m seeing:
- small: 2–4 GPU nodes, <100 RPS
- medium: 8–20 GPU nodes, 100–500 RPS
- large: 50+ nodes, >500 RPS
Prometheus is mostly cluster metrics + runtime, not per-request.

Thomas Baird: Quick rule of thumb that’s worked: TSDB footprint ~ 1–3 bytes/sample on disk (varies), plus head/WAL overhead. In K8s installs, people underestimate churn (pods, containers, series churn). I’d budget 2x for head + compaction + WAL.

Sean Gallagher: Do we have any datapoints from our staging private cluster? Like actual series count + disk/day?

Sahana Iyer: From the smoke-test harness (single cluster, 3 serving pods, control plane, kube-state-metrics, node exporter) I saw ~180k active series once it settles. Disk growth was ~1.5–2.0GB/day at 15s scrapes w/ 7d retention, but that cluster is tiny.

Rafael Mendes: That’s helpful. Most customer clusters will have more nodes + more container metrics. Also some will turn on additional exporters (ingress-nginx, etc.).

Allison Grant: We should explicitly call out scrape interval expectations. If we’re assuming 15s and customer sets 5s, our sizing falls apart.

Connor O'Brien: Packaging-wise: helm values today default to 15s scrape in kube-prometheus-stack. We can document that changing it requires re-sizing PVC.

Thomas Baird: Another thing: enable `--storage.tsdb.wal-compression` by default. Helps w/ WAL size for write-heavy setups.

Logan Wright: +1 on WAL compression. Also I’m in favor of setting both:
- `--storage.tsdb.retention.time=7d`
- `--storage.tsdb.retention.size=80GB` (or similar)
So time-based retention doesn’t fill disks if there’s unexpected cardinality.

Sean Gallagher: What should the PVC be then? If we cap size at 80GB but PVC is 50Gi, we’re going to thrash earlier.

Thomas Baird: Prometheus needs free space for compaction and block writes. If you set retention.size near the PVC limit, you can still OOM/lock up on compaction.
Rule I use: retention.size <= ~60–70% of PVC. Rest is head/WAL/compaction buffer.

Rafael Mendes: Support reality: customers don’t want Prometheus consuming >200Gi unless we justify it.
Can we do defaults like:
- small: 50Gi PVC, 7d, retention.size 30Gi
- medium: 200Gi PVC, 15d, retention.size 120Gi
- large: 500Gi+, 15–30d depending on remote write
?

Allison Grant: That seems reasonable. For the minimum pack, we probably ship one default (small/medium-ish) and then provide a sizing table.

Diego Santos: I’d rather we ship a single default that won’t blow up on "medium". Maybe:
- default PVC: 100Gi
- default retention.time: 7d
- default retention.size: 60Gi
Then document: if you want 15d, go 200–300Gi.

Logan Wright: 100Gi default might be too big for some on-prem (local PVs are annoying), but too small for large clusters. Still probably the least-worst.

Sahana Iyer: FWIW, some air-gapped POCs have *tiny* storage classes. We got a question last week: “can I run this with 20Gi?”

Sean Gallagher: 20Gi feels like guaranteed pain unless you’re scraping almost nothing.

Thomas Baird: Yeah 20Gi will fill in a couple days once kube metrics are in. Also if storage is slow, compaction gets backed up.
We should add a note: “use SSD-backed storage; avoid networked HDD for Prometheus TSDB if possible.”

Rafael Mendes: Also: what about Grafana? People ask for "Grafana storage" too. It’s mostly sqlite + plugins. We can say 5–10Gi PVC is enough.

Allison Grant: +1. Grafana persistent volume mostly for dashboards/provisioning state. If dashboards are provisioned from configmaps, you can even run ephemeral, but let’s keep 10Gi default to be safe.

Connor O'Brien: In helm we already provision dashboards as configmaps. Grafana PVC is optional, but enabling persistence avoids losing user-created dashboards. Minimum pack probably shouldn’t assume custom dashboards though.

Sean Gallagher: OK, proposing we bake into doc + values:
Prometheus:
- PVC: 100Gi default
- retention.time: 7d
- retention.size: 60Gi
- wal-compression: true
And we publish a table (small/medium/large) with recommended PVC/retention.

Thomas Baird: I’d add one more knob: set requests/limits for Prometheus so it doesn’t get evicted under pressure. CPU spikes during compaction.
Maybe requests: 2 CPU / 8Gi mem, limits: 4 CPU / 16Gi mem for the default footprint.

Rafael Mendes: +1, we’ve seen OOMKills in customer clusters when compaction runs and memory is too low.

Sahana Iyer: In CI, Prometheus w/ 2Gi mem was flakey. 8Gi request stabilized it.

Allison Grant: Can we also include “cardinality guardrails” section: if series > X, you should reduce labels or drop high-churn metrics? That’ll help Support.

Logan Wright: Yes. Maybe a quick snippet to check series count:
`prometheus_tsdb_head_series`
And churn:
`rate(prometheus_tsdb_head_series_created_total[5m])`

Sean Gallagher: Great. I’ll update the sizing guidance draft with:
- default values
- sizing table (small/med/large)
- formulas + series/churn queries
- callouts about scrape interval + SSD storage

Connor O'Brien: Please also add a one-liner for installer preflight: warn if Prometheus PVC < 50Gi when min pack enabled.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: and add "if disk usage >85% page" guidance + runbook link. That’s the common incident path.

Sean Gallagher: ack. I’ll also add that `retention.size` should be <70% of PVC to leave compaction headroom.

Thomas Baird: One nit: Gi vs GB confusion. In values, it’s Gi. In docs, call it out explicitly.

Allison Grant: +1. People misconfigure and then wonder why it’s tight.

Sean Gallagher: Cool, I’ll push an update today and drop link here for review.

Sean Gallagher: (later) Draft updates are in the doc: added table + default `100Gi / 7d / 60Gi`. Please comment if you think 100Gi is too aggressive for default installs.

Rafael Mendes: Reviewed. Looks good. I added a comment to emphasize remote_write as the path for >15d retention instead of bumping TSDB forever.

Logan Wright: +1 on remote_write note. Otherwise folks treat Prometheus as their long-term store.

Allison Grant: Left minor edits on wording + added the series/churn queries. Approved from standards POV.

Connor O'Brien: From packaging POV: OK. Preflight warning threshold at 50Gi makes sense.

Sean Gallagher: :shipit: I’ll proceed with the defaults + docs and we can tweak if staging noise comes back.

Maybe requests: 2 CPU / 8Gi mem, limits: 4 CPU / 16Gi mem for the default footprint.

Rafael Mendes: +1, we’ve seen OOMKills in customer clusters when compaction runs and memory is too low.

Sahana Iyer: In CI, Prometheus w/ 2Gi mem was flakey. 8Gi request stabilized it.

Allison Grant: Can we also include “cardinality guardrails” section: if series > X, you should reduce labels or drop high-churn metrics? That’ll help Support.

Logan Wright: Yes. Maybe a quick snippet to check series count:
`prometheus_tsdb_head_series`
And churn:
`rate(prometheus_tsdb_head_series_created_total[5m])`

Sean Gallagher: Great. I’ll update the sizing guidance draft with:
- default values
- sizing table (small/med/large)
- formulas + series/churn queries
- callouts about scrape interval + SSD storage

Connor O'Brien: Please also add a one-liner for installer preflight: warn if Prometheus PVC < 50Gi when min pack enabled.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: and add "if disk usage >85% page" guidance + runbook link. That’s the common incident path.

Sean Gallagher: ack. I’ll also add that `retention.size` should be <70% of PVC to leave compaction headroom.

Thomas Baird: One nit: Gi vs GB confusion. In values, it’s Gi. In docs, call it out explicitly.

Allison Grant: +1. People misconfigure and then wonder why it’s tight.

Sean Gallagher: Cool, I’ll push an update today and drop link here for review.

Sean Gallagher: (later) Draft updates are in the doc: added table + default `100Gi / 7d / 60Gi`. Please comment if you think 100Gi is too aggressive for default installs.

Rafael Mendes: Reviewed. Looks good. I added a comment to emphasize remote_write as the path for >15d retention instead of bumping TSDB forever.

Logan Wright: +1 on remote_write note. Otherwise folks treat Prometheus as their long-term store.

Allison Grant: Left minor edits on wording + added the series/churn queries. Approved from standards POV.

Connor O'Brien: From packaging POV: OK. Preflight warning threshold at 50Gi makes sense.

Sean Gallagher: :shipit: I’ll proceed with the defaults + docs and we can tweak if staging noise comes back.

title: #eng-infra: 1741254992
preview:
Sahana Iyer: We need to nail down CI secrets + signing keys for the runtime LTS release branches. Right now the release workflows can build fine, but publishing + signing is blocked because the secrets are only scoped to main and we don’t want to just “enable secrets on release/*” and call it a day. Any suggestions on reducing blast radius but still letting us publish LTS artifacts? (context: INT-3302)

Devon Price: +1. For LTS patch week we’ll need:
- push runtime images to registry (packages:write)
- generate checksums + provenance
- sign images + checksums (cosign + whatever we decide for tarballs)
We can’t put long-lived keys into repo secrets accessible from PR contexts.

Connor O'Brien: Release Eng POV: I’m fine with an extra manual approval step if it keeps us out of trouble. The patch cadence is monthly; we can live with “protected environment + required reviewers” as long as it doesn’t require someone from Security to wake up every time.

Sean Gallagher: SRE concern is more about emergency patches. If we have a Sev1/CVE, we can’t be blocked for hours because a single person holds the keys. We need a path that’s still controlled but not a single point of failure.

Sahana Iyer: Current state (GitHub Actions):
- Workflows run on `push` to `runtime-1.18-lts` and tags
- We have `RUNTIME_SIGNING_KEY` and `REGISTRY_TOKEN` stored as GH secrets in the repo (long-lived)
- These are disabled for anything except main to avoid accidental exposure
So release branch workflows can’t sign/publish.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Please don’t widen long-lived secret scope to “release/*” with no guardrails. We’ll get dinged in the next audit. We need (a) least privilege, (b) strong audit trail of who approved key use, and ideally (c) short-lived creds.

Devon Price: Can we go keyless signing for containers? Cosign supports OIDC-based signing via GitHub, no private key stored.

Hanae Suzuki: Keyless for containers is good *if* we can still provide customers a stable verification story. Some regulated customers will want an explicit identity policy (“only tags in org/repo, from workflow X”) and clear docs.

Connor O'Brien: Enterprise asks have included “signed artifacts + checksums” and “traceable to commit.” They didn’t explicitly require offline verification, but they *did* ask for auditable provenance.

Sahana Iyer: We still have non-container artifacts for Private: e.g. the installer wants a checksum file, sometimes a tarball of runtime bits. We need a signing story for those too.

Bruno Silva: For Private installer pinning (PR #663), we can validate checksums easily, but customers keep asking “who signed the checksum manifest.” So we probably need to sign `SHA256SUMS` (or similar) with a key we can rotate.

Sean Gallagher: Suggestion: split it into two tracks:
1) Container images: keyless cosign w/ GitHub OIDC + SLSA provenance
2) Checksum manifest: sign with KMS-backed key (no raw key in CI)

Rafael Mendes: +1. For emergencies we can keep the same mechanism but use an “emergency” environment that has fewer reviewers (still >1) and stricter alerting.

Devon Price: GitHub Environments could work:
- `runtime-lts-release` environment
- required reviewers: Release Eng on-call + one Infra approver
- secrets only in env
- only allow deploys from `runtime-*-lts` branches + tag refs
That keeps secrets out of PR runs.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Environments help, but I’d still prefer eliminating static secrets where possible. If we can do AWS/GCP KMS via OIDC, then CI never sees the raw key. Also gives CloudTrail-style audit logs.

Sahana Iyer: We’re on AWS for most CI infra. We can use `id-token: write` and assume role via GitHub OIDC. That role can:
- call KMS Sign for the checksum manifest key
- push to ECR (or our registry) using short-lived creds
No more long-lived registry token.

Devon Price: That’s a bigger change but seems right. What about the “sign images” step? We can use keyless cosign with OIDC without AWS at all, but if we want KMS-backed cosign key we can do that too.

Hanae Suzuki: Keyless is fine for images IMO. For the checksum manifest, KMS-backed signing gives customers a clean artifact: `SHA256SUMS` + `SHA256SUMS.sig` and we can publish the public cert / verification instructions.

Connor O'Brien: My request is: keep patch flow simple. Release managers can click “Approve deployment” in GH when we’re ready to publish.

Sean Gallagher: Also need to limit who can trigger workflows. We’ve had weird “someone reran old workflow” incidents.

Sahana Iyer: Implementation sketch:
- Release workflow only runs on `workflow_dispatch` (manual) for publish/sign steps
- Build/test can still run on push
- Publish job uses environment `runtime-lts-release`
- Environment requires approval by (Release Eng rotation) + (Infra on-call)
- Publish job uses OIDC -> AWS AssumeRole
- That role has: minimal ECR push + KMS Sign only for specific key ARN
- No repo-level long-lived secrets

Devon Price: That works. We should ensure the build artifacts are immutable between build and publish. Otherwise “manual publish” could sign something different.

Sahana Iyer: Yep. Plan is:
- Build job uploads artifacts to GH Actions artifacts with SHA pinned
- Publish job downloads by run ID, verifies commit SHA + tag matches
- Then generates manifest, signs, and uploads to release bucket/registry

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Add a control: the signing job should assert it’s running from a tag like `runtime-1.18.1-lts` and that the tag points to a commit on the LTS branch. Otherwise someone can tag random commits.

Connor O'Brien: We already planned tag validation in the release manager checklist (PR #23488). We can automate it in CI too.

Bruno Silva: For Private installer, we also need stable URLs and checksum file layout. Please don’t change file names every patch. :)

Rafael Mendes: Emergency path: do we want an `runtime-lts-security` environment with a different reviewer set? In an incident, we may not want to wait for a specific infra approver.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: I’m okay with an emergency env if:
- still 2-person approval (no single approver)
- it pages Security on use
- it’s documented + reviewed post-incident

Sean Gallagher: +1. Paging Security on “security env used” is fine.

Devon Price: Action items:
1) Sahana to prototype OIDC->AWS role for release signing + registry push
2) Devon to update the release workflow to split build vs publish (manual + protected environment)
3) Aisha/Hanae to confirm signing approach for `SHA256SUMS` (KMS algo, verification story)

GitHub Actions: Devon Price added a reminder: "Draft env protection rules for runtime-lts-release" (due in 2 days)

Sahana Iyer: I’ll spin up the role + policy today. Proposed permissions:
- `ecr:GetAuthorizationToken`, `ecr:BatchCheckLayerAvailability`, `ecr:PutImage`, etc scoped to `runtime-lts/*`
- `kms:Sign` scoped to key `alias/runtime-lts-checksums`
- deny everything else
Also will set `permissions:` in workflow to least required.

Hanae Suzuki: For KMS signing: recommend RSA_PSS_SHA_256. Then we can verify with OpenSSL in docs. (We’ll need to publish the public key / cert somewhere stable.)

Connor O'Brien: Cool. Please also note: we need this to work for `runtime-1.18-lts` and future `runtime-1.19-lts` without a bunch of one-off IAM changes.

Sahana Iyer: We can pattern-match branch names in the workflow, but IAM scoping can be by repo + workflow identity (subject claim) instead of branch-specific. For AWS OIDC conditions, we can lock it to:
- repo: `redwood/redwood`
- workflow file: `.github/workflows/runtime-release.yml`
- ref: `refs/tags/runtime-*-lts` for the publish job
That should scale across branches.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: That’s the right direction. Please avoid `pull_request_target` entirely for this. Also: we should rotate/disable the existing long-lived secrets once OIDC path is verified.

Devon Price: :thumbsup: I’ll open a PR for the workflow changes and tag y’all.

Sean Gallagher: When it’s ready, let’s do a tabletop for “security hotfix patch” to ensure the env approvals + paging don’t deadlock us.

Connor O'Brien: +1. I’ll add to the LTS runbook and schedule a short drill after we merge.

title: #eng-infra: 1740648811
preview:
Harish Reddy: hey folks 👋 quick review request. runbook draft for provision-to-ready failures is in Confluence. before we mark it “oncall-ready” i want eyes on remediation ordering. right now it says “quarantine -> reimage -> drain” which feels wrong / risky.

Harish Reddy: link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGINFRA/Provision-to-ready+failures+triage+runbook (WIP)

Vanessa Ortiz: yeah +1, that order can cause ugly surprises. if you reimage before drain/cordon, kube still thinks it has a node + we can get stuck terminating pods / misreported capacity. also if node is in serving pool, you can take a partial outage.

Rafael Mendes: +1 from SRE side. we should make the “safe default” path explicit. oncall should never start with reimage unless we’ve already removed it from traffic and captured artifacts.

Gabe Wilson: agreed. the orchestrator will also keep retrying unless we flip state + stop retries. we need to be clear what’s authoritative: readiness service state vs kube node state.

Sahana Iyer: also: reimage is not reversible. if the issue is image mismatch vs transient NCCL flake, we lose the data we need (agent bundle / journalctl) to diagnose.

Harish Reddy: ok so proposed new order is: cordon/drain -> quarantine -> reimage? or quarantine -> cordon/drain -> reimage?

Vanessa Ortiz: i’d write it as a numbered “Do this in order” section depending on where the node is in the lifecycle:
1) If node is already attached to a serving pool: remove from serving pool / ensure it’s not receiving traffic (control-plane gate should prevent this but reality 🙃)
2) `kubectl cordon` the node
3) Drain with safe flags
4) Quarantine in readiness service (so orchestration stops and it shows up in tooling)
5) Collect artifacts (validation agent bundle, dmesg, nvidia-smi, kubelet logs)
6) Reimage (only after above)

Rafael Mendes: +1. quarantine should happen before reimage, but doesn’t have to be before drain. i’d prefer quarantine right after cordon, so we stop any automatic retries + the UI reflects “operator engaged”.

Gabe Wilson: same. orchestrator sees Quarantined and won’t retry validation steps.

Keiko Yamamoto: can we specify drain flags? we’ve had people do plain drain and it hangs forever on daemonsets.

Vanessa Ortiz: yep. something like:
`kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --grace-period=60 --timeout=10m --force`
…with a note: if it times out, stop and follow “Drain stuck” subsection (don’t keep force-draining blindly).

Aisha Bello: + please add “before you drain, check if it’s holding any system pods we care about” (node-validation-agent DS is fine, but if it’s running anything else unexpected, call it out). also link the dashboard panel for “nodes in Validating > 30m” so oncall can verify impact.

Miles Thornton: runbook should be explicit about *when to quarantine* vs *when to retry validation*. e.g. if it’s a known flake signature (NCCL timeout) first do a single retry from orchestrator UI, then quarantine after N attempts.

Harish Reddy: current text says “retry twice then quarantine”. we can make it more concrete per step.

Connor O'Brien: release/ops note: reimage on a whole batch during rollout week can backfire (capacity shortfall). can we add a warning: limit parallel reimages per pool/region and coordinate in #eng-releases if it’s > X nodes?

Vanessa Ortiz: yes. add guardrails:
- max 2 concurrent reimages per pool (unless Harish/Vanessa approve)
- if quarantine count > 5% of pool, stop rollout + page Infra lead

Fiona McCarthy: security angle: please add “don’t ssh around ad-hoc / don’t disable hardening” as a quick callout. if troubleshooting requires elevated access, use breakglass procedure and leave an audit trail. also “collect artifacts” should not include copying customer data; keep it to system logs + validation outputs.

Sahana Iyer: also in the “image mismatch” section, add: confirm node image channel/version tag matches pool (candidate vs stable). we’ve seen folks reimage to ‘latest’ and that breaks pinning.

Gabe Wilson: +1. we have audit events for image channel changes now. runbook should say: don’t change image channel as a remediation unless you’re the oncall for releases + you’ve got approval.

Harish Reddy: ok, i’ll update the runbook with:
- new ordering: cordon -> quarantine -> drain -> artifact bundle -> reimage
- a separate branch: “if node never joined cluster” (no drain possible)
- clear drain flags + timeout guidance
- “limit blast radius” section

Rafael Mendes: small tweak: cordon then *drain*, then quarantine is fine too, but if quarantine triggers automated deprovision in some future, we might prefer quarantine after drain. what does quarantine do today?

Gabe Wilson: today quarantine = readiness service state + label in kube + taint that blocks scheduling (but cordon also blocks). it does not auto-terminate instance.

Vanessa Ortiz: in that case cordon -> quarantine -> drain is ok. quarantine early helps make it visible in console.

Aisha Bello: please add explicit “verification” steps after each action. e.g.
- After cordon: `kubectl get node <n> -o jsonpath='{.spec.unschedulable}'`
- After quarantine: check readiness UI shows Quarantined + failure reason
- After drain: `kubectl get pods -A --field-selector spec.nodeName=<n>` should be empty (except DS)

Harish Reddy: got it.

Connor O'Brien: and add an “escalation” box: if drain stuck > 15m or quarantine spike, page Infra + SRE (owner rotations) vs just looping.

Fiona McCarthy: + add link to breakglass doc + remind people not to paste full logs in public channels (use secure paste / attach to ticket).

Harish Reddy: will do. i’ll push edits today and re-request review.

Vanessa Ortiz: tag me on the updated page. also please remove the existing sentence “reimage is always safe and fastest” 😅

Harish Reddy: lol noted, deleting that line now.

Aisha Bello: once updated, i’ll add dashboard links + make sure the runbook references the right panel names (avoid “click around until you find it”).

Rafael Mendes: thanks. once we have the order fixed, SRE can sign off for oncall enablement.

Harish Reddy: ✅

Rafael Mendes: small tweak: cordon then *drain*, then quarantine is fine too, but if quarantine triggers automated deprovision in some future, we might prefer quarantine after drain. what does quarantine do today?

Gabe Wilson: today quarantine = readiness service state + label in kube + taint that blocks scheduling (but cordon also blocks). it does not auto-terminate instance.

Vanessa Ortiz: in that case cordon -> quarantine -> drain is ok. quarantine early helps make it visible in console.

Aisha Bello: please add explicit “verification” steps after each action. e.g.
- After cordon: `kubectl get node <n> -o jsonpath='{.spec.unschedulable}'`
- After quarantine: check readiness UI shows Quarantined + failure reason
- After drain: `kubectl get pods -A --field-selector spec.nodeName=<n>` should be empty (except DS)

Harish Reddy: got it.

Connor O'Brien: and add an “escalation” box: if drain stuck > 15m or quarantine spike, page Infra + SRE (owner rotations) vs just looping.

Fiona McCarthy: + add link to breakglass doc + remind people not to paste full logs in public channels (use secure paste / attach to ticket).

Harish Reddy: will do. i’ll push edits today and re-request review.

Vanessa Ortiz: tag me on the updated page. also please remove the existing sentence “reimage is always safe and fastest” 😅

Harish Reddy: lol noted, deleting that line now.

Aisha Bello: once updated, i’ll add dashboard links + make sure the runbook references the right panel names (avoid “click around until you find it”).

Rafael Mendes: thanks. once we have the order fixed, SRE can sign off for oncall enablement.

Harish Reddy: ✅

title: #eng-infra: 1740259101
preview:
Logan Wright: Heads up / quick retro from the Sev1 earlier — telemetry query latency spiked hard right when incident-bot started pulling saturation cards.

Grafana Bot: Alert triggered: telemetry-query-service p95_latency_ms > 5000 for 10m (current: 8120ms). Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/telemetry-query-service

Rafael Mendes: +1, oncall perspective: bot was timing out and then responders started manually refreshing dashboards -> made it worse. Need guardrails.

Miles Thornton: I pulled logs from incident-bot. We went from ~3 qps to ~70 qps in ~2 mins when people hit “Refresh signals” in multiple threads. Also Slack interactive actions retried a few times when ack was late.

Devon Price: ack deadline for Slack interactive is 3s; we were doing live query before ack in a couple paths. That causes retry storms. We can flip to ack-immediately + async update, but still need query cost controls.

Nadia Rahman: Looking at query service traces — the expensive ones are the “regional saturation overview” fanout queries. They expand to ~18 metric series per region * pool * model, and then we do 5m + 30m windows. During the incident, cardinality was already elevated.

Caleb Johnson: Yep, and some are uncached due to tenant param being part of cache key even when query is region-level. So we miss cache and re-run same query N times.

Jacob Stein: This aligns with what we saw: bot card mostly needs the *summary* (e.g., GPU util high + queue depth high + KV cache pressure) and then links to deeper dashboards. We shouldn’t compute the whole dashboard query set inline.

Aisha Bello: +1. Also: we should define a query budget for incident-bot per thread + per workspace. If it exceeds budget, bot should fall back to cached context and post deep links.

Allison Grant: For “fallback to links” behavior, can we standardize the deep links and time windows? During incident I saw people open dashboards with wrong time range.

Dylan Brooks: Proposal:
1) Add request coalescing in telemetry-query-service for identical queries (keyed by normalized query + time bucket).
2) Add cache with small TTL (30-60s) + stale-while-revalidate for bot use.
3) Enforce hard limits: max fanout, max series returned, and max query time (e.g., 2s) for bot scope.
4) If any limit hits: return partial + “degraded mode” payload with links.

Rafael Mendes: What do we mean by “normalized query”? We need to ensure it collapses equivalent requests across threads. Eg same region/service but different incident id.

Caleb Johnson: Normalize away incident_id/thread_id; round end_time to nearest 30s bucket; enforce fixed lookback windows for bot (5m, 30m). Also canonicalize label filters ordering.

Nadia Rahman: Another thing: query service currently lets callers ask arbitrary window. Bot sometimes requested 2h because it used “incident start” which changed as people edited the incident doc. Should pin to a fixed set of windows.

Miles Thornton: On bot side: we can remove “Refresh signals” from v2 cards during active incident unless you’re in a whitelisted oncall role. Or throttle it (1 refresh / 60s / thread).

Devon Price: We should also make refresh idempotent + ignore repeated clicks while one is in-flight. Slack UI feels like “nothing happened” so folks click 5x.

Logan Wright: I’m also worried about the query service being in the same blast radius. During incidents, telemetry is often degraded. Bot should not depend on it being healthy to be useful.

Jacob Stein: Exactly. For saturation card we can do:
- Prefer cached last-known-good for region/pool summary
- If live query >2s, stop + show cached + “data may be stale”
- Always attach links to dashboards + traces/logs for drilldown

Allison Grant: Re links: we have agreed formats in the incident-context guidelines draft. Needs to include service, region, and an explicit time window (now-30m..now). Also a “copy query” for power users.

Aisha Bello: Also want alerts tuned so we don’t page on the *telemetry tool* being slow unless it impacts SLO diagnosis broadly. But we do need a canary to detect when bot queries are slow (separate from generic query load).

Dylan Brooks: There’s already a ticket in flight for caching/coalescing (ENG-5427). We should add explicit “bot budget” enforcement + circuit breaker semantics.

Nadia Rahman: Circuit breaker idea: if p95 > X for Y minutes, query service returns 429-like “budget exceeded” to bot scope, and bot flips to link-only mode automatically. Avoid death spiral.

Rafael Mendes: +1. In the incident, responders were basically DDoSing telemetry without knowing. A clear message in the bot card would have helped.

Incident Bot: :warning: Some diagnostics may be incomplete due to telemetry query latency. Try dashboard links or rerun later.

Miles Thornton: Can we make that message more actionable?
Something like: “Telemetry is slow (p95 8s). Showing cached signals from 2m ago. For live data: open Saturation Overview dashboard (region prefilled).”

Devon Price: Yes. Also: we should ack Slack instantly and then update message when ready. If it’s degraded, update with that explanation. No retries.

Caleb Johnson: One more culprit: a couple queries were pulling scheduler backpressure with high-cardinality labels (fallback routing tags). I saw series count jump 10x. There was a recent hotfix PR to reduce that cardinality; ensure it’s deployed everywhere.

Logan Wright: Noted. I’ll check rollout status w/ release eng. Also, action items below.

Logan Wright: Action items (proposed):
- Telemetry team (Nadia/Caleb): implement query normalization + coalescing + TTL cache + stale-while-revalidate for bot scope. Add max-series + max-fanout + max-runtime enforcement. (maps to ENG-5427 + new subtask)
- Bot team (Miles/Devon/Dylan): ack-first, in-flight dedupe, per-thread refresh throttle, and degraded-mode messaging w/ links. (maps to ENG-5425 + ENG-5429)
- Observability (Allison/Aisha): confirm link formats/time windows + add perf canary for bot query latency and alert route. (maps to perf-canary PR)

Aisha Bello: Looks good. For budgets, can we define something concrete?
E.g. per incident thread: <= 10 live queries / 5 min; per workspace: <= 100 live queries / 5 min; else cached/links.

Nadia Rahman: Reasonable. We should enforce server-side too (query service) so bots can’t bypass.

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also separate budgets for tenant-aware queries (more expensive) vs region-level.

Rafael Mendes: For SRE: please add a snippet to oncall playbook: if bot shows degraded mode, don’t spam refresh; use links; if needed, run manual CLI which is rate-limited separately.

Jacob Stein: I’ll update the runbook section for “telemetry degraded” + what the saturation card means when cached.

Allison Grant: I’ll sync with Chloe on Console deep links. Need to ensure links land on the right region/pool and default to now-30m.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll open the additional Linear subtask for query budgets + degraded-mode contract between bot and query service.

Logan Wright: Thanks all. Let’s aim to have the cache/coalesce + bot throttle in before v2 broad rollout. This was a good warning shot.

title: #eng-infra: 1739738455
preview:
Irene Choi: Trying to close out the provisioning checklist deltas for Premium tier today. Need crisp differences vs Plus/Standard: warm pool, spare nodes, HA. Can folks sanity check?

Irene Choi: Draft deltas (Premium only):
- Warm pool (pre-warmed) capacity maintained in-region
- N+1 spare node(s) in the Dedicated pool
- HA config (multi-AZ) + failover runbook validated
- Additional perf validation (burn-in)

Vanessa Ortiz: +1 on having it explicit. For warm pool: can we define as “1 node equivalent per region per Premium customer” or is it per shape class?

Jamal Pierce: per customer warm pool is gonna be rough in us-east if they pick H100. Supply is tight. I’d rather define as “warm pool availability subject to shape availability” but that gets contract-y.

Harish Reddy: Operationally we can do warm pool as “one spare *slot*” that can be any compatible node, not necessarily identical GPU SKU. But perf/SLO implications.

Nina Petrova: If it’s not the same SKU, failover can violate their throughput floor. For Premium we should keep warm pool same shape family (e.g., H100 8x stays H100 8x).

Rafael Mendes: From SRE side: HA story needs to be clear. Premium implies we *actually* test failover (not just have Terraform). Checklist item should include “simulate node loss + verify throughput stays above floor within X mins”.

Keiko Yamamoto: Re: spare nodes, are we saying N+1 per deployment (so if customer has 2 nodes we maintain 1 spare) or 1 spare minimum regardless? Autoscaler can take advantage but cost is real.

Vanessa Ortiz: For Premium deployments we’ve been assuming minimum 1 spare per deployment, and if >4 nodes then 20% spare (rounded up). But that’s not written anywhere.

Irene Choi: Ok. Let’s translate into checklist language, not policy language.

Irene Choi: Proposed Premium checklist items:
1) Warm pool:
   - Confirm warm pool reservation exists in same region + same shape family
   - Node(s) are pre-imaged + driver/runtime pinned to current Dedicated baseline
   - Run “warm validation” (model load + first token latency smoke)

2) Spare capacity:
   - Ensure at least 1 spare node capacity available for deployment (same shape family)
   - Confirm spare is in “ready” state (joined cluster, health checks passing, not serving traffic)

3) HA:
   - Deploy spans >=2 AZs (or failure domains if provider doesn’t map to AZ)
   - Validate quorum/etcd/control plane redundancy
   - Execute failover drill: drain one node + kill one node, confirm routing + throughput floor recovery within 10 min

4) Burn-in:
   - Run 30 min burn-in at target concurrency using Redwood perf harness
   - Capture tokens/sec + p95 latency + GPU util; attach to handoff ticket

Thoughts?

Nina Petrova: Looks good. On HA: add explicit “networking” check: cross-AZ bandwidth + security group rules for NCCL. We’ve had surprises where multi-AZ is configured but comms are throttled.

Nina Petrova: Suggest add:
- Validate interconnect: `ibstat`/`nvidia-smi topo -m` + bandwidth smoke
- Verify ingress LB health checks per AZ

Harish Reddy: +1. Also add “spare node is on a separate rack/failure domain if available”. Doesn’t always apply but we can do best-effort.

Keiko Yamamoto: Warm pool “pre-imaged + model load smoke” is key. I’d add a requirement: warm pool nodes keep KV cache disabled / cleared. We don’t want weird cache state from previous canary loads.

Rafael Mendes: On failover drill, I’d like it to include “customer-facing endpoint still returns 2xx” plus “no auth/rate-limit regressions” (we’ve seen miswired policies on secondary).

Connor O'Brien: Release angle: please add “feature flags for tier-aware routing enabled” + “fallback policy set to Premium defaults” before handoff. Otherwise they get provisioned as Premium but routed like Plus.

Vanessa Ortiz: For the spare node, make sure the checklist clarifies it’s capacity in the *Dedicated pool* not just ‘available in region’. Sales keeps asking for “spare guaranteed” and we need to define it as in-pool.

Irene Choi: Ack. Updating checklist with these additions:
- HA networking/interconnect validation
- Failover drill includes 2xx + policy validation
- Tier routing flags + fallback policy verification
- Spare node explicitly “in-pool”

Jamal Pierce: One more note: if Premium is multi-AZ, provisioning lead time is longer (IP blocks, subnets, quota). Can we add “confirm cloud quota (EIPs/NICs/IB) before promising date” as a Premium-only precheck?

Vanessa Ortiz: yes please. We should add a preflight section: quota + capacity + AZ availability.

Irene Choi: Good call. Adding Premium preflight:
- Confirm shape capacity in >=2 AZs
- Confirm quota: GPU, IB/NVSwitch (if relevant), EIP/NIC, local SSD
- Confirm warm pool + spare node reservation can be met

Rafael Mendes: Also include: “page SRE for failover drill scheduling” or at least “notify oncall window”. We don’t want random chaos testing during peak.

Irene Choi: I’ll add “schedule failover drill in agreed window; notify #eng-oncall + attach run command output”.

Nina Petrova: Can we standardize the commands we want attached?
- `kubectl get nodes -o wide`
- `nvidia-smi -q -x | head`
- perf harness summary
- routing config snapshot

Irene Choi: Yep. I’ll include a snippet in the checklist.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: once you paste final, I’ll drop into the runbook doc.

Connor O'Brien: +1. Also please tag me in the runbook PR so release checklist stays consistent.

Irene Choi: Final draft deltas for Premium provisioning checklist (vs Plus/Standard):
PRE-FLIGHT (Premium)
- Confirm target region has shape capacity in >=2 AZs/failure domains
- Confirm cloud quotas: GPU, networking (NIC/EIP), storage, IB/NVSwitch where applicable
- Confirm warm pool reservation available (same region + same shape family)
- Confirm spare node capacity available *in Dedicated pool* (same shape family)

PROVISIONING (Premium)
- Multi-AZ deployment (or equivalent failure domains) + control plane redundancy validated
- Networking/interconnect validated (NCCL path not degraded; cross-AZ bandwidth smoke)
- Tier-aware routing enabled + Premium fallback policy configured

VALIDATION (Premium)
- Warm pool nodes pre-imaged, pinned runtime/driver baseline, model load smoke completed
- Spare node is “ready” (cluster-joined, health checks passing, not serving)
- Burn-in: 30 min at target concurrency; capture tokens/sec + p95 latency + GPU util
- Failover drill scheduled/ran: drain + kill node; confirm endpoint 2xx + policy intact; throughput floor recovers within 10 min

ARTIFACTS TO ATTACH
- `kubectl get nodes -o wide`
- `nvidia-smi topo -m` + `nvidia-smi`
- perf harness summary output
- routing/tier config snapshot

If no objections in next ~1h I’ll consider it locked.

Vanessa Ortiz: looks solid. no objections.

Nina Petrova: good.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1.

Rafael Mendes: works. thanks.

Harish Reddy: ship it.

Connor O'Brien: :shipit: tag me on the runbook update.
